$comment: Locked at Phase 1.2. Hand-curated multi-version catalogue of frameworks the Splunk Monitoring Use Cases catalogue maps against. Each framework has 1..n versions. Each version carries its own clause_grammar, clause_examples, clause_url_template, common_clauses[] with priority_weights, and pending_changes[]. derives_from graph propagates parent mappings to derivative frameworks with per-clause divergences. Authoritative sources are ingested separately under data/crosswalks/olir and data/crosswalks/oscal; this file is the project-level index. See LEGAL.md for the disclaimer and per-source licence attributions. Replaces data/regulations.draft.json, which remains only for Phase 0.2 reproducibility. v1.1.0 (2026-04-20, Regulation-to-UC Story Redesign Phase 1): each commonClauses[] entry MAY additionally carry optional 'obligationText' (string, 40-600 chars, the regulator's own requirement in plain-but-faithful language) and 'obligationSource' (URL, deep link to the regulator-published paragraph the obligationText was extracted from). Backfilled tier-by-tier; consumed by api/v1/compliance/clauses/*.json, api/v1/compliance/story/*.json, and the clause-navigator / compliance-story HTML surfaces.

priorityWeightRubric.$comment: Weights derived from regulator-published priority language. Rubric: 1.0 = the regulator uses 'must' / mandatory / baseline-required; 0.7 = the regulator uses 'should' or includes this as a moderate-baseline control; 0.4 = 'may' / enhancement / addressable / recommended; 0.2 = informative / example / appendix.

commonClauses.topic: Data protection by design and by default

commonClauses.obligationText: Taking into account the state of the art, the costs of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of processing as well as the risk of varying likelihood and severity for the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the controller and the processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including inter alia as appropriate pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data; the ability to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of processing systems and services; the ability to restore availability and access to personal data in a timely manner in the event of a physical or technical incident; and a process for regularly testing, assessing and evaluating the effectiveness of technical and organisational measures for ensuring the security of the processing.

In the case of a personal data breach, the controller shall without undue delay and, where feasible, not later than 72 hours after having become aware of it, notify the personal data breach to the supervisory authority competent in accordance with Article 55, unless the personal data breach is unlikely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons. Where the notification to the supervisory authority is not made within 72 hours, it shall be accompanied by reasons for the delay.

versions.grammarNotes: OTCC clause numbering follows the NCA convention 'Domain-Subdomain-MainControl-SubControl' (e.g. OTCC-2-12-1-1 = Domain 2 Cybersecurity Defence, Subdomain 12 Event Logs and Monitoring Management, Main control 1, Subcontrol 1). The 'OTCC-' prefix is canonical to this catalogue; the source document uses plain numeric IDs (e.g. '2-12-1-1'). Subdomain numbering mirrors the publicly available NCA Essential Cybersecurity Controls (ECC-1:2018) which OTCC-1:2022 derives from; UCs referencing OTCC clauses are tagged 'requires_sme_review: true' until a Saudi-licensed CISSP/ISO-Lead-Auditor cross-references the NCA-published OTCC PDF (which is access-controlled via the NCA portal).

commonClauses.topic: OT cybersecurity policy approval and communication
commonClauses.obligationText: The organisation must define, document, approve, publish, and communicate cybersecurity policies and procedures specifically tailored to its Operational Technology environment. Policies must be reviewed at least annually, after any major OT change, and following any cybersecurity incident affecting OT systems. Approval must be at the level of the senior executive responsible for cybersecurity.

The organisation must implement a cybersecurity risk management methodology covering all OT assets. Risks must be identified, assessed, treated, and monitored on a continuous basis, with the OT risk register reviewed at least annually and updated after any material change to the OT environment or its threat landscape.

commonClauses.topic: OT personnel cybersecurity awareness and training
commonClauses.obligationText: Personnel with access to OT systems must receive role-specific cybersecurity awareness training before being granted access and at least annually thereafter. Training records must be maintained for the duration of the employment plus the OTCC retention period and produced on demand to NCA inspectors.

The organisation must conduct internal cybersecurity reviews of OT systems at planned intervals (at least annually) and independent external assessments at least every three years. Audit findings must be tracked through a corrective-action programme with documented closure evidence retained for audit.

The organisation must establish and maintain an accurate, comprehensive, and continuously reconciled inventory of all OT assets, including hardware, software, firmware, configurations, and network connections. Each asset must be classified by its criticality to safe and reliable operation and assigned a documented owner.

Privileged access to OT systems must be uniquely identified, time-limited, approved, recorded with full session video or keystroke logging, and reviewed for legitimacy. Shared, default, or vendor accounts must be disabled or rotated; emergency break-glass accounts must follow a documented use-and-review procedure.

commonClauses.topic: Vendor and third-party remote access to OT
commonClauses.obligationText: Remote access by vendors or third parties to OT systems must be authorised on a per-session basis, mediated by an organisation-managed jump host, multi-factor authenticated, restricted to the minimum systems and time required, and fully recorded. Access must be revoked immediately on completion.

The organisation must establish documented secure-configuration baselines for each class of OT system (HMI, engineering workstation, historian, PLC, RTU, SIS controller). Baselines must be enforced, drift from baseline must be detected within defined thresholds, and any deviation must be either remediated or formally accepted with documented compensating controls.

All changes to OT systems must be subject to documented change-control including risk assessment, impact analysis on safety and reliability, formal approval, scheduling within an approved maintenance window, and post-change validation. Unauthorised configuration changes must be detected by automated means and treated as potential security incidents.

OT endpoints capable of supporting it must have malware-protection technologies installed and kept current. Where endpoint anti-malware cannot run on OT hosts (legacy controllers, real-time devices), compensating network-level detection must be deployed with documented justification and risk acceptance.

The use of removable media (USB, optical, portable storage) on OT systems must be either disabled by default or controlled through an approved-device allowlist, with all attempted use logged. Any media introduced to OT must be malware-scanned on a dedicated transfer station before use.

commonClauses.topic: Wireless access controls on OT networks
commonClauses.obligationText: Wireless technologies in OT environments must be inventoried, restricted to documented business need, configured with strong cryptographic protocols, segmented from corporate wireless, and continuously monitored for unauthorised access points and clients.

commonClauses.topic: OT network segmentation and zone enforcement
commonClauses.obligationText: The OT network must be logically and physically segmented from the corporate IT network via a documented demilitarised zone or equivalent, with traffic between zones restricted to explicitly approved flows. Internal OT zones must follow the Purdue Enterprise Reference Architecture or an equivalent zone-conduit model with enforcement by stateful inspection or industrial firewalling.

commonClauses.topic: OT cryptographic controls and key management
commonClauses.obligationText: Cryptographic controls (TLS endpoints, OPC UA secure channels, web-management consoles on OT firewalls, switches, HMI servers, and vendor management planes) must use approved algorithms and key sizes, certificates must be valid and issued by a trusted Certificate Authority, and keys must be rotated within the documented cryptoperiod. Cryptographic key management procedures must include issuance, distribution, escrow, revocation, and destruction, with auditable evidence retained for inspection. Plant-floor protocols that do not natively support cryptography must be compensated by segmentation per OTCC-2-5-3-1 and physical access controls per OTCC-2-14-1-1.

commonClauses.topic: OT backup integrity and recovery testing
commonClauses.obligationText: OT system backups (including controller programs, HMI projects, historian data, and engineering configurations) must be taken at intervals matching the documented recovery-point objective, stored in a manner that resists corruption and ransomware (immutable / air-gapped / offline), and restoration tested at least annually with documented evidence.

The organisation must operate an OT vulnerability management programme covering passive discovery (no active scanning of safety-critical components without explicit risk assessment), vendor advisory ingestion, prioritisation by criticality and exposure, and time-bound remediation with risk-acceptance documentation for compensating controls.

commonClauses.topic: OT patch management with safety validation
commonClauses.obligationText: OT patch deployment must follow a documented procedure including vendor approval, staging on a representative test bed, validation against safety and process performance, deployment within a planned maintenance window, and post-deployment verification. Where patching is not feasible, compensating controls must be documented and reviewed.

commonClauses.topic: OT event logging completeness and retention
commonClauses.obligationText: Cybersecurity events from OT systems and their supporting infrastructure must be logged, centrally collected, synchronised to a reliable time source, protected against tampering, and retained for a minimum of twelve months online and the OTCC archival period offline. Logs must be reviewed at defined cadences and during incident handling.

Industrial protocol traffic (such as Modbus/TCP, DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104, EtherNet/IP, OPC UA, PROFINET) must be monitored on critical conduits for protocol-level anomalies including unauthorised function codes, unexpected device-to-device flows, and command injection patterns. Detections must feed the cybersecurity event management process.

commonClauses.topic: OT-tier phishing and email-borne threat detection
commonClauses.obligationText: Engineering, operations, and other OT-adjacent personnel must be protected by phishing-resistant email controls, and indicators of compromise from email-borne threats must be correlated with subsequent activity on OT-adjacent endpoints (engineering workstations, jump hosts) to detect attempted pivots into OT.

commonClauses.topic: OT cybersecurity incident detection and classification
commonClauses.obligationText: The organisation must operate continuous OT cybersecurity monitoring with documented detection criteria, classify incidents by severity and impact on safe operation, escalate per a documented IR plan, and conduct post-incident review with lessons learned fed back into OT defensive controls.

commonClauses.topic: OT cybersecurity incident reporting to NCA
commonClauses.obligationText: Material OT cybersecurity incidents must be reported to the NCA within the timeframe specified in NCA notification guidelines (typically within 72 hours of confirmation, with an initial notification within 24 hours for incidents of national impact). Reports must include classification, impact assessment, immediate containment steps, and a remediation timeline.

commonClauses.topic: Physical access control to OT environments
commonClauses.obligationText: Physical access to control rooms, instrument racks, marshalling cabinets, network enclosures, and any space containing OT equipment must be restricted to authorised personnel, recorded by access-control or visitor-logging systems, and reconciled to badge / credential inventories. Tailgating, propped doors, and after-hours access must be detected.

Safety Instrumented Systems must be protected against cyber compromise that could degrade their independent-protection-layer function. SIS networks must be segregated from BPCS networks, bypass-switch activations must be authorised and logged, and any program or configuration change must be subject to functional-safety management of change as well as cybersecurity change control.

The organisation must operate a business continuity programme that covers cyber-induced loss of OT availability or integrity, with exercises conducted at least annually that include realistic ransomware, destructive attack, and supply-chain disruption scenarios. Lessons learned must be documented and fed into the OT cybersecurity programme.

commonClauses.topic: OT recovery time and recovery point objectives
commonClauses.obligationText: The organisation must define, agree with business owners, and continuously measure recovery-time and recovery-point objectives for each critical OT system. Actual recovery performance against RTO and RPO must be evidenced from recovery tests and real incidents.

commonClauses.topic: Third-party and supply-chain cybersecurity assurance for OT
commonClauses.obligationText: Cybersecurity requirements aligned with OTCC must be contractually imposed on any third party that designs, builds, operates, maintains, or supplies OT components or services. Compliance must be evidenced through periodic assessment, right-to-audit, and incident-reporting obligations on the supplier.

commonClauses.topic: OT cloud and hosting cybersecurity assurance
commonClauses.obligationText: Where OT-supporting workloads (historian aggregation, analytics, asset management, threat intelligence) are deployed on cloud or external hosting, the cloud arrangement must comply with NCA Cloud Cybersecurity Controls (CCC) and the organisation must retain control over data residency, encryption keys, and incident-response coordination with the cloud provider.

frameworks.name: Security of Critical Infrastructure Act 2018 (Cth) and Critical Infrastructure Risk Management Program Rules 2023
aliases: Security Legislation Amendment (Critical Infrastructure Protection) Act 2022

versions.grammarNotes: SOCI clauses in this catalogue have three families. 'SOCI-s<N><suffix>' refers to a section of the Security of Critical Infrastructure Act 2018 (Cth) as amended by SLACIP 2022 (e.g. SOCI-s30AC is Part 2A s 30AC 'Duty to adopt and maintain a critical infrastructure risk management program'). 'SOCI-CIRMP-r<N>(.<sub>)?' refers to a Rule (or sub-Rule) of the Security of Critical Infrastructure (Critical Infrastructure Risk Management Program) Rules (LIN 23/006) 2023 — e.g. CIRMP-r6.1 is Rule 6, subrule (1) covering cyber-and-information-security material risks. 'SOCI-ECSO-<slug>' refers to Enhanced Cyber Security Obligations for Systems of National Significance (SoNS) declared under s 52B, drawing from CISC ECSO guidance. 'SOCI-cross-<slug>' covers Australia-specific cross-cutting expectations (data residency, OT segmentation, audit-evidence retention) that the Cyber and Infrastructure Security Centre (CISC) raises during CIRMP reviews. Clause identifiers and obligation text are written for the consolidated 'as-amended' Act and the in-force CIRMP Rules 2023; pending amendments and pending SoNS declarations are tracked in pendingChanges.

commonClauses.obligationText: A responsible entity for a critical infrastructure asset must provide and keep current the information required for the Register of Critical Infrastructure Assets, including operational information about the asset and information about each direct interest holder. Updates must be lodged within 30 days of any material change. The register is maintained by the Secretary of the Department of Home Affairs under Part 2 and supports Government Assistance Powers under Part 3A.

commonClauses.topic: Duty to adopt and maintain a Critical Infrastructure Risk Management Program (CIRMP)
commonClauses.obligationText: The responsible entity for a critical infrastructure asset to which Part 2A applies must adopt, maintain, and comply with a written Critical Infrastructure Risk Management Program (CIRMP) that identifies each material risk that could have a relevant impact on the asset and that minimises or eliminates each material risk so far as it is reasonably practicable to do so. The CIRMP must cover cyber-and-information-security hazards, personnel hazards, supply-chain hazards, and physical-and-natural hazards.

The responsible entity must review the CIRMP regularly and at least once each financial year, must update the CIRMP if a hazard or material risk emerges or materially changes, and must record both the review and any updates. The review must be supported by an evidence base sufficient to demonstrate that each identified material risk is still being minimised or eliminated so far as is reasonably practicable.

commonClauses.topic: Board (or equivalent) approval and annual report
commonClauses.obligationText: The CIRMP, and each annual report on the CIRMP, must be approved by the board of the responsible entity (or, where the entity has no board, by the equivalent governing body or accountable officer). The annual report must address whether the CIRMP was up to date during the year, whether any hazard had a significant relevant impact, the variations made to the program, and any non-compliance.

If a responsible entity becomes aware that a cyber-security incident is having a significant impact on the availability of a critical infrastructure asset, the entity must give the Australian Signals Directorate (ASD) a notification of the incident as soon as practicable, and in any event within 12 hours after becoming aware. Notification may be oral; a written notification covering the same content must follow within 84 hours under s 30BF.

If a responsible entity becomes aware that a cyber-security incident has had, is having, or is likely to have a relevant impact (but not a significant impact) on a critical infrastructure asset, the entity must notify the ASD of the incident within 72 hours of becoming aware. A written follow-up notification must be provided within 84 hours under s 30BF if the original notification was oral.

If a notification under s 30BC or s 30BD was given orally, the responsible entity must give the ASD a written notification covering the same information within 84 hours of the original notification. The written follow-up must include all material facts known to the entity at the time and identify the responsible officer.

commonClauses.topic: Statutory cyber-incident response plan for Systems of National Significance (SoNS)
commonClauses.obligationText: The responsible entity for a System of National Significance must, when required by written notice from the Minister under s 30CB, adopt, maintain, and comply with a written statutory cyber-incident response plan for the SoNS. The plan must specify the actions the entity will take when, and the people responsible for taking those actions when, a cyber-security incident occurs or is imminent. The plan must be kept up to date and complied with at all times.

commonClauses.topic: Cyber-security exercise on demand for SoNS
commonClauses.obligationText: The Secretary may, by written notice, require the responsible entity for a System of National Significance to undertake one or more cyber-security exercises (tabletop, scenario, or simulated) within a specified period. The entity must comply and must give the Secretary an evaluation report on each exercise within the timeframe set in the notice.

commonClauses.topic: Vulnerability assessment on demand for SoNS
commonClauses.obligationText: The Secretary may, by written notice, require the responsible entity for a System of National Significance to undertake one or more vulnerability assessments of the SoNS within a specified period and to give the Secretary an assessment report. ASD officers may, with consent or under s 30DJ, install software on the SoNS to undertake the assessment.

commonClauses.topic: System information periodic reporting (event-log telemetry) for SoNS
commonClauses.obligationText: The Secretary may require the responsible entity for a SoNS to provide system-information periodic reports (event-log telemetry that is needed to detect a cyber-security incident) to the ASD in the form and at the intervals specified in the notice. The entity must produce the information in the agreed format and using the agreed transport, and must continue to do so until the notice is revoked.

commonClauses.topic: CIRMP Rules — General requirements and material-risk identification
commonClauses.obligationText: A responsible entity's CIRMP must establish and maintain a process or system to identify each hazard that could give rise to a material risk to the availability, integrity, reliability, or confidentiality of the asset, must assess each identified material risk against the impact criteria in the Rule, and must record the basis for each assessment. Material risks identified must be re-assessed when circumstances change and at least annually.

Rule 6(1) requires the CIRMP to establish and maintain a process or system to minimise or eliminate material risks of unauthorised access to, modification of, or interference with computer systems or data that supports the operation of the critical infrastructure asset, so far as is reasonably practicable. The process must address material risks of disclosure of sensitive operational data, denial of service to operational systems, and compromise of the integrity of operational data.

Rule 6(2) requires the responsible entity to establish and maintain a process or system to detect cyber-security incidents in respect of the critical infrastructure asset, including continuous monitoring of relevant computer systems and data, and to respond to and recover from those incidents. The entity must also test the effectiveness of those detection, response, and recovery arrangements at least annually.

commonClauses.topic: CIRMP Rules — Mandatory cyber framework adoption (Aug 2024 deadline)
commonClauses.obligationText: By 17 August 2024 (and on a continuing basis thereafter), the responsible entity must be compliant with a recognised cyber-security framework — one of the Australian Cyber Security Centre's Essential Eight Maturity Model (maturity level 1 or higher), AS ISO/IEC 27001:2022, NIST Cybersecurity Framework 1.1 or later, the 2020-21 AESCSF framework, the C2M2 maturity model, or an equivalent framework approved by the Secretary. Compliance evidence must be maintained and produced on request.

commonClauses.topic: CIRMP Rules — Personnel hazards: critical-worker identification and assessment
commonClauses.obligationText: Rule 7 requires the CIRMP to establish and maintain a process or system to identify each critical worker for the critical infrastructure asset (a person whose absence or compromise would have a relevant impact on the asset) and to minimise or eliminate the material risk that a critical worker poses to the asset through screening, background checks, and access management proportionate to the role.

commonClauses.topic: CIRMP Rules — Personnel hazards: insider-threat detection and removal
commonClauses.obligationText: Rule 7 requires the CIRMP to establish and maintain a process or system to minimise or eliminate the material risk that a malicious insider, negligent insider, or coerced insider poses to the asset. This includes monitoring critical-worker activity for anomalous behaviour, having a documented process to revoke access immediately on termination or risk indication, and rehearsing the removal process at least annually.

commonClauses.topic: CIRMP Rules — Supply-chain hazards: vendor risk register
commonClauses.obligationText: Rule 8 requires the CIRMP to establish and maintain a process or system that identifies and minimises or eliminates material risks that supply-chain arrangements (including managed-service providers, OT vendors, and key software suppliers) pose to the critical infrastructure asset. The entity must maintain a register of supply-chain dependencies, must assess each dependency for material risk, and must apply controls proportionate to the risk including contract requirements, attestations, and right-to-audit.

commonClauses.topic: CIRMP Rules — Supply-chain hazards: continuous supplier monitoring
commonClauses.obligationText: The supply-chain process must include continuous monitoring of the cyber and operational risk posture of suppliers identified as material — through threat-intelligence feeds, vendor security ratings, breach disclosures, and product-vulnerability advisories — and must trigger CIRMP re-assessment when a supplier's risk profile materially changes.

commonClauses.topic: CIRMP Rules — Physical and natural hazards: site security
commonClauses.obligationText: Rule 9 requires the CIRMP to identify and minimise or eliminate material risks from physical hazards (unauthorised access, theft, vandalism, sabotage) to the asset's operating sites, including OT control rooms, plant areas, communications rooms, and supporting infrastructure. Access to these areas must be authorised, recorded, and reviewed for legitimacy.

commonClauses.topic: CIRMP Rules — Natural hazards and business continuity exercises
commonClauses.obligationText: Rule 9 also requires the CIRMP to identify and minimise or eliminate material risks from natural hazards (fire, flood, severe weather, seismic events) and to test the entity's response and recovery arrangements at least annually through documented exercises. Exercises must cover both IT and OT failure modes and must produce a written evaluation that drives improvement.

commonClauses.topic: CIRMP Rules — Annual report to the Department
commonClauses.obligationText: Rule 10 requires the responsible entity to give the Department (or, for ASD-administered sectors, the relevant Commonwealth regulator) an annual report on the CIRMP within 90 days after the end of the financial year. The report must be board-approved and must include a description of any material change to the CIRMP during the year, whether the CIRMP was up to date during the year, any significant hazard impact, the entity's compliance status, and an attestation by the accountable officer.

commonClauses.topic: Enhanced Cyber Security Obligations — vulnerability disclosure and remediation tracking
commonClauses.obligationText: Where the Minister has declared a System of National Significance, the responsible entity must establish a documented process to receive, triage, and remediate vulnerability disclosures affecting the SoNS — covering ASD advisories, Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure submissions, supplier security bulletins, and exploits in the wild. Remediation status must be tracked against a Service-Level Agreement aligned to the asset's relevant impact threshold and demonstrated on request to the Secretary under s 30CM.

commonClauses.topic: OT zone-and-conduit segmentation (Defence, Energy, Water, Critical Manufacturing)
commonClauses.obligationText: Responsible entities for OT-bearing critical-infrastructure assets must enforce, monitor, and evidence segmentation between corporate IT and operational technology. The CISC interprets Rule 6(1) to require documented zones and conduits aligned with ISA/IEC 62443-3-2, technical enforcement at the IT/OT boundary (firewall, data diode, or DMZ), continuous monitoring for cross-zone traffic that violates the documented allow-list, and an exception register for any approved cross-zone flow.

commonClauses.topic: OT asset inventory with criticality classification (CISC expectation)
commonClauses.obligationText: The CISC expects responsible entities to maintain a continuously reconciled OT asset inventory — covering PLCs, RTUs, HMIs, engineering workstations, SIS controllers, network infrastructure, and embedded sensors — with each asset classified by criticality to safe and reliable operation. The inventory underpins the asset register reported under s 18 and the material-risk identification under Rule 5.

commonClauses.topic: Australian data residency and operational-data sovereignty
commonClauses.obligationText: Responsible entities should ensure that operational data and protected security information generated by, or relating to, a critical infrastructure asset is stored, processed, and accessed in a manner consistent with the asset's criticality and the s 35AC Protected Information regime — typically meaning primary storage in Australia, with offshore storage permitted only where specifically authorised under the entity's risk-management approach and accompanied by contractual and technical controls preserving Commonwealth access rights.

commonClauses.topic: Encryption of operational data in transit between zones
commonClauses.obligationText: The CISC expects responsible entities to encrypt operational data in transit between trust zones, between operational sites, and between operational sites and corporate IT — using cryptographic primitives consistent with ACSC's Information Security Manual (ISM) cryptography guidance (TLS 1.2+, AES-256, or other ASD-Approved Cryptographic Algorithms) and to monitor for downgrade, certificate expiry, and weak-cipher negotiation as a material-risk indicator under Rule 6(1).

commonClauses.topic: Audit-evidence retention for CIRMP and incident reporting
commonClauses.obligationText: Records produced under the CIRMP (material-risk register, control evidence, board approvals, annual reports), records produced under the cyber-incident reporting obligations (s 30BC / s 30BD / s 30BF), and records produced under Enhanced Cyber Security Obligations must be retained for at least 7 years (consistent with the Archives Act 1983 and CISC guidance) and produced on demand to the Department, ASD, or a CIRMP-authorised auditor.

frameworks.name: America's Water Infrastructure Act of 2018 (Section 2013 amendments to SDWA s 1433)

versions.grammarNotes: AWIA section numbering refers to the Safe Drinking Water Act (SDWA) 42 U.S.C. § 300i-2 as amended by AWIA s 2013. 'AWIA-s1433<lower>(<n>)' refers to SDWA 1433 subsection, paragraph, and clause (e.g. AWIA-s1433a(1) is SDWA 1433(a)(1), the Risk and Resilience Assessment requirement). 'AWIA-RRA-<slug>' refers to the Risk and Resilience Assessment topical scope expected by EPA in its Baseline Information on Malevolent Acts and the J100-21 / AWWA M19 / VSAT-Web guidance. 'AWIA-ERP-<slug>' refers to the Emergency Response Plan strategy requirement under s 1433(b). 'AWIA-EPA-<slug>' refers to EPA Cybersecurity Programmatic guidance, the CWC (Cybersecurity for Water Conveyance / WaterISAC) reporting workflow, and the EPA Memorandum 'Addressing Cybersecurity in PWS Sanitary Surveys' (March 2023, vacated by court 2023, replaced by July 2024 Cybersecurity Action Plan guidance — see pendingChanges).

commonClauses.topic: Risk and Resilience Assessment (RRA) — duty and scope
commonClauses.obligationText: Each community water system serving more than 3,300 persons must conduct an assessment of the risks to, and resilience of, its system. The RRA must include the risk to the system from malevolent acts and natural hazards; the resilience of the pipes and constructed conveyances, physical barriers, source water, water collection and intake, pretreatment, treatment, storage and distribution facilities, electronic, computer, or other automated systems (including the security of such systems); the monitoring practices of the system; the financial infrastructure of the system; the use, storage, or handling of various chemicals by the system; and the operation and maintenance of the system.

commonClauses.topic: RRA submission deadlines by system size
commonClauses.obligationText: Each community water system must certify completion of its Risk and Resilience Assessment to the EPA Administrator on the statutory deadline applicable to its size band (>100,000: 31 March 2020; 50,000-99,999: 31 December 2020; 3,301-49,999: 30 June 2021) and must review and revise the RRA at least every 5 years. EPA does not retain the RRA document — only the certification — but the system must retain the RRA for the same 5-year window for inspection.

commonClauses.topic: Emergency Response Plan (ERP) — duty and content
commonClauses.obligationText: No later than 6 months after certifying completion of the RRA, each community water system must prepare or revise an Emergency Response Plan that incorporates findings from the RRA. The ERP must include strategies and resources to improve resilience of the system, including the physical security and cybersecurity of the system; plans and procedures that can be implemented in the event of a malevolent act or natural hazard; actions, procedures, and equipment that can mitigate the impact of such events; and strategies that can be used to aid in the detection of such events.

commonClauses.topic: ERP coordination with state, local, tribal, and territorial partners
commonClauses.obligationText: Each community water system must, to the extent possible, coordinate its Emergency Response Plan with existing local emergency planning committees and with state, local, tribal, and territorial emergency response and homeland-security officials. Coordination must be documented and the ERP must be revised when external arrangements materially change.

Each community water system must certify to the EPA Administrator that it has conducted the RRA on or before the relevant statutory deadline, and within 6 months thereafter must certify that it has prepared or revised the ERP. Certifications are submitted via EPA's electronic AWIA portal; signed paper certifications mailed to the AWIA Coordinator are accepted only by exception.

commonClauses.topic: Baseline Information on Malevolent Acts — EPA-defined cyber and physical threat set
commonClauses.obligationText: EPA's Baseline Information on Malevolent Acts of Relevance to Community Water Systems (2019, refreshed 2022) enumerates the threat set that community water systems must consider in the RRA. The cyber subset covers ransomware against SCADA / control systems / business networks, insider threats to chemical-dosing controls, manipulation of distribution-system pressure / flow / chlorine residual, denial-of-service against billing or SCADA, exfiltration of customer data, and supply-chain compromise of OT components.

commonClauses.topic: Natural hazards in the RRA — flood, drought, wildfire, seismic, severe weather
commonClauses.obligationText: The RRA must consider natural-hazard risk to source water, treatment, and distribution including flood, drought, wildfire, seismic events, severe weather, and climate-change-driven shifts in any of these. Hazard data must be sourced from authoritative providers (USGS, NOAA, FEMA, state hazard mitigation plans, EPA Climate Resilience Evaluation and Awareness Tool / CREAT).

commonClauses.topic: RRA — electronic, computer, and automated systems (cyber scope)
commonClauses.obligationText: The RRA must specifically assess the resilience and security of the system's electronic, computer, and automated systems — historically interpreted by EPA and AWWA as covering enterprise IT, SCADA (HMI, controller, historian, engineering workstation), PLC / RTU at treatment plants and lift stations, plant network infrastructure, billing and customer-portal systems, and remote-access pathways used by operators, vendors, and third-party engineering firms.

commonClauses.topic: RRA — monitoring practices (continuous data quality and integrity)
commonClauses.obligationText: The RRA must assess monitoring practices including water-quality online analysers, distribution-system pressure / flow sensors, residual-chlorine analysers, source-water intake monitoring, and the SCADA / historian / lab-information chain that records and reports their values. The integrity of monitoring data — including detection of stuck values, suppressed alarms, falsified readings, or operator-shielded data — is treated by EPA and AWWA as a primary cyber-resilience concern.

commonClauses.topic: RRA — chemical storage, handling, and dosing
commonClauses.obligationText: The RRA must specifically assess the use, storage, and handling of treatment chemicals (chlorine gas, sodium hypochlorite, polymers, coagulants, fluoride, lime, hydrofluosilicic acid, sodium hydroxide, soda ash) including dosing automation, dosing setpoint authority, and the cyber-controllable pathways by which a malicious actor could over-dose, under-dose, or stop dosing.

The RRA must consider the financial infrastructure of the system — billing systems, customer payment portals, e-commerce gateways, vendor-payment systems — and the impact of a cyber-incident or natural hazard on revenue continuity, payroll, and emergency procurement. EPA's J100-21 guidance treats financial-system disruption as a material AWIA risk where it impairs the utility's ability to operate.

commonClauses.topic: ERP — strategies, resources, and actions to mitigate identified risks
commonClauses.obligationText: The Emergency Response Plan must enumerate strategies and resources to improve resilience to each material risk identified in the RRA, including physical security and cybersecurity. Strategies must be assigned to a named role, must list the resources (personnel, equipment, contracts, mutual-aid) required for execution, and must specify the actions that will be taken when the risk materialises.

commonClauses.topic: ERP — detection strategies for malevolent acts and natural hazards
commonClauses.obligationText: The ERP must describe the strategies that the system uses to detect malevolent acts and natural hazards including SCADA / OT alarms, water-quality online monitoring, perimeter intrusion detection, video surveillance, public-tip lines, social-media listening, and external feeds from WaterISAC / EPA / CISA. The ERP must specify how detection signals are escalated to incident response.

The ERP must describe procedures for responding to a cyber-incident including initial triage, isolation of affected systems, manual operation fallback, customer and regulator notification (Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure Act / CIRCIA where applicable, state primacy agency, EPA Region, WaterISAC), recovery, and lessons-learned. Procedures must be exercised at least annually.

commonClauses.topic: ERP — mutual-aid coordination (WARN networks and local partners)
commonClauses.obligationText: The ERP must reference Water and Wastewater Agency Response Network (WARN) memberships and the activation criteria for mutual aid, and must list the contacts and authorities for state and local emergency operations centres, state primacy agencies, EPA Regional Water Protection Coordinators, and CISA / FBI for cyber incidents.

commonClauses.topic: ERP — review and revision every 5 years (and after material change)
commonClauses.obligationText: Each community water system must review and revise its Emergency Response Plan every 5 years, aligned with the RRA review cycle, and after any material change to the system or its operating environment (significant infrastructure changes, post-incident lessons learned, new regulatory or threat-intelligence guidance from EPA or WaterISAC).

commonClauses.topic: Cyber-incident reporting — WaterISAC and EPA Region pathway
commonClauses.obligationText: EPA strongly encourages community water systems to report cyber-incidents to WaterISAC (the Information Sharing and Analysis Center for the water sector) and to the EPA Regional Water Protection Coordinator as soon as practicable after detection. From October 2023, CISA's Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure Act (CIRCIA) reporting overlay applies; once the final CIRCIA rule is in force, water systems will additionally have a 72-hour reporting duty to CISA for covered cyber-incidents.

commonClauses.topic: EPA Cybersecurity Action Plan — voluntary cyber-incorporation into sanitary surveys
commonClauses.obligationText: EPA's July 2024 Cybersecurity Action Plan (which replaces the vacated March 2023 Memorandum 'Addressing Cybersecurity in PWS Sanitary Surveys') invites states to incorporate cybersecurity assessment into sanitary surveys of public water systems. Where a state has adopted this approach, the PWS must demonstrate basic cyber hygiene during the sanitary survey including network segmentation, remote-access controls, authentication, patching, backup, and incident response.

commonClauses.topic: EPA / CISA Top Cyber Actions for Water Utilities (Top 8 / 9 / Pathway)
commonClauses.obligationText: EPA's 'Top Actions for Securing Water Systems' and CISA's 'Pathway to Cybersecurity for the Water and Wastewater Sector' identify a minimum cyber baseline: reduce internet exposure, conduct regular cybersecurity assessments, change default passwords immediately, inventory OT/IT assets, develop and exercise cybersecurity incident response and recovery plans, back up systems and data, reduce exposure to vulnerabilities, conduct cybersecurity awareness training, and implement multi-factor authentication on all remote access. AWIA RRAs and ERPs that ignore these are routinely flagged in EPA audits.

commonClauses.topic: Use of recognised RRA methodology — J100-21 / AWWA M19 / VSAT-Web
commonClauses.obligationText: EPA does not mandate a specific RRA methodology but accepts (and audits against) ANSI/AWWA / ASCE-ASME-AWWA J100-21 Risk and Resilience Management of Water and Wastewater Systems, AWWA Manual M19 Emergency Planning for Water and Wastewater Utilities, and EPA's free Vulnerability Self Assessment Tool web edition (VSAT-Web). RRAs that document their methodology against one of these standards are treated as on-foundation.

EPA's Top Actions and CISA's Pathway both require a continuously maintained inventory of OT and IT assets — including SCADA, PLCs, HMIs, RTUs at remote lift stations / wells / tanks, network infrastructure, internet-exposed services, and remote-access endpoints — as the prerequisite to vulnerability management, patch management, and incident response.

commonClauses.topic: Multi-factor authentication on all remote access
commonClauses.obligationText: EPA's Top Actions and CISA's Pathway require MFA on every remote-access pathway into the utility including VPN, jump host, vendor-engineering remote access to PLCs / HMIs / engineering workstations, SCADA cloud portals, and management interfaces. Single-factor remote access is treated as a material AWIA risk and is the most common finding in published CISA water-sector advisories.

commonClauses.topic: Network segmentation between IT and OT
commonClauses.obligationText: EPA and CISA expect documented, enforced, and monitored segmentation between corporate IT and OT (SCADA, PLC, instrumentation) networks. Acceptable enforcement patterns include industrial firewall / data diode / DMZ with restricted protocols and a continuously monitored allow-list. Direct internet exposure of SCADA, HMI, or engineering workstations is a leading published cause of water-sector cyber incidents.

commonClauses.topic: System and data backup and tested recovery
commonClauses.obligationText: EPA and CISA require utilities to back up SCADA configurations, PLC programs, historian data, water-quality data, and business systems on a regular schedule, to retain offline / immutable copies, and to test restoration at least annually so that manual operation or full system rebuild is achievable within the ERP recovery time objective.

commonClauses.topic: Change default passwords on all OT and IT devices
commonClauses.obligationText: EPA and CISA require utilities to identify and change default passwords on all OT and IT devices including PLCs, HMIs, network equipment, IP cameras, building-management systems, and engineering workstations on commissioning. CISA's published water-sector intrusion advisories repeatedly identify unchanged vendor default credentials as the initial-access vector — usually via internet-exposed HMI or VNC.

EPA's Top Actions require utilities to deliver cybersecurity awareness training to all employees with system access — including operators, engineers, administrators, contractors, and board members — and to refresh the training at least annually. Training must cover phishing, MFA hygiene, removable-media risk, OT-specific physical-tampering recognition, and incident-reporting workflow.

commonClauses.topic: Reduce exposure to vulnerabilities — patching and configuration
commonClauses.obligationText: EPA and CISA require utilities to track known vulnerabilities in OT and IT (CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog, vendor advisories, ICS-CERT bulletins), to remediate or apply documented compensating controls within a defined SLA proportionate to operational risk, and to retain evidence of remediation. OT patching must include safety validation before deployment.

Each community water system must retain a copy of the RRA, the ERP, and the EPA certification for at least 5 years from the date of certification (one full review cycle) and must produce these on request to EPA in connection with sanitary surveys, AWIA compliance reviews, or post-incident enquiries.

pendingChanges.subject: CIRCIA covered-cyber-incident reporting (CISA — proposed rule April 2024)
pendingChanges.status: Proposed final rule pending; final rule expected late 2025 / 2026. Once in force, water systems (CWS and wastewater systems serving more than 3,300 people are within the proposed covered-entity scope) will have a 72-hour duty to report covered cyber-incidents to CISA, layered on top of AWIA and EPA reporting pathways.

pendingChanges.subject: EPA cyber-in-sanitary-survey — July 2024 Cybersecurity Action Plan
pendingChanges.status: EPA's July 2024 Cybersecurity Action Plan invites state primacy agencies to voluntarily incorporate cybersecurity into PWS sanitary surveys. The March 2023 EPA Memorandum that previously made this mandatory was vacated in October 2023 by the Eighth Circuit. Where a state has voluntarily adopted, PWS audits will include cyber.

frameworks.name: Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure Act of 2022 (CIRCIA) + CISA 2024 NPRM (proposed final rule)
aliases: Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure Act

versions.grammarNotes: Clauses follow three families. (1) 'CIRCIA-s2242<lower>(<n>)(<letter>)' refers to the statutory text of the Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure Act of 2022 codified at 6 U.S.C. § 681b et seq. (Division Y of Public Law 117-103). (2) 'CIRCIA-NPRM-<slug>' refers to the CISA Notice of Proposed Rulemaking published April 4, 2024 (89 FR 23644) covering covered-entity scope, covered-incident definition, the 72-hour reporting clock, the 24-hour ransom-payment reporting clock, supplemental reporting, data and records retention, and CIRCIA Agreements. (3) 'CIRCIA-CISA-<slug>' refers to CISA's interim reporting guidance, the Cyber Incident Reporting Council pathways, and the relationship to existing federal reporting (NRC, FCC, FDA, FAA, FERC, SEC, EPA, FBI / Secret Service, sectoral SROs).

commonClauses.obligationText: CIRCIA defines 'covered entity' as an entity in a critical infrastructure sector (as defined in Presidential Policy Directive 21) that satisfies the definition established by CISA in the implementing regulations. 'Covered cyber incident' means a substantial cyber incident experienced by a covered entity that satisfies the definition established by CISA. Both definitions are operationalised by the April 2024 NPRM and will be finalised in the Final Rule.

commonClauses.topic: Mandatory covered-cyber-incident report — within 72 hours of reasonable belief
commonClauses.obligationText: A covered entity that experiences a covered cyber incident must report the incident to CISA not later than 72 hours after the covered entity reasonably believes that the covered cyber incident has occurred. Reports must contain the information specified in 6 U.S.C. § 681b(c) including incident description, affected systems, suspected threat-actor identity, vulnerabilities exploited, security defences in place, mitigation and recovery actions, and contact information for the covered entity.

A covered entity that makes a ransom payment as the result of a ransomware attack against the covered entity must report the payment to CISA not later than 24 hours after the ransom payment has been made. Reports must contain the information specified in 6 U.S.C. § 681b(d) including ransomware variant, payment instructions, payment amount and crypto address, threat-actor identity if known, and incident scope. The ransom-payment reporting duty applies even if the underlying incident is not a 'covered cyber incident'.

commonClauses.topic: Supplemental report — when new material information emerges
commonClauses.obligationText: A covered entity that has reported a covered cyber incident must submit supplemental reports to CISA promptly when substantial new or different information about the incident emerges, including additional or revised information related to the items in the original report, until the incident is fully mitigated or otherwise resolved. The duty to submit supplemental reports applies until the covered entity notifies CISA that the incident has concluded and full recovery is achieved.

commonClauses.topic: Records preservation — preserve data and records related to the incident
commonClauses.obligationText: A covered entity that has submitted a covered cyber-incident report or a ransom-payment report must preserve the data and records relevant to the incident in accordance with the requirements established by CISA in the implementing regulation. The NPRM proposes a 2-year preservation duty from the date of the most recent report on the incident.

Reports submitted under CIRCIA are protected: a covered entity may not be held liable in any cause of action solely for submitting a report; reports are not discoverable in civil cases; and reports are protected from FOIA and state public-records requests. Reports do not constitute a waiver of any applicable privilege or protection (attorney-client, work product, trade secret).

commonClauses.topic: Enforcement — request for information and subpoena authority
commonClauses.obligationText: If a covered entity does not submit a required report, CISA may issue a request for information (RFI). If the covered entity does not respond within 72 hours of the RFI, CISA may issue an administrative subpoena. Failure to comply with the subpoena may be referred to the Attorney General for civil enforcement in US District Court; the Attorney General may seek penalties including contempt and civil fines as authorised by law.

The April 2024 NPRM proposes that 'covered entity' includes entities in critical infrastructure sectors that meet either of: (a) entities exceeding the SBA small-business size standard in the relevant North American Industry Classification System (NAICS) code, or (b) entities that meet sector-based criteria for high-impact within their sector (e.g. CWSs serving more than 3,300 persons; chemical facilities of interest to CISA; healthcare entities with more than 100 ICU beds; certain financial services entities; certain transportation entities). The Final Rule is expected to refine these criteria.

commonClauses.topic: Covered-incident definition (proposed in 2024 NPRM) — 'substantial' cyber incident
commonClauses.obligationText: The April 2024 NPRM proposes that a 'covered cyber incident' is a substantial cyber incident experienced by a covered entity that results in any of: (a) substantial loss of confidentiality, integrity, or availability of information systems; (b) serious impact on the safety and resiliency of operational systems and processes; (c) disruption of business or industrial operations; (d) unauthorised access to information systems facilitated through compromise of a cloud service provider, managed service provider, or other third-party data hosting provider.

The 72-hour clock starts at the time the covered entity 'reasonably believes' a covered cyber incident has occurred. The NPRM clarifies that 'reasonably believes' is an objective standard met when the entity has gathered enough information to support a good-faith determination that the incident is in scope; it does not require certainty or full incident scoping. Entities must build internal procedures to surface this determination point with timestamp evidence.

The 24-hour ransom-payment clock starts when the ransom payment is made (broadcast to the blockchain for crypto, or initiated from the bank for fiat). The clock applies to all covered entities, regardless of whether the underlying ransomware attack would itself trigger covered-cyber-incident reporting. Insurance carrier or incident-response retainer making a payment on behalf of the covered entity does not shift the reporting duty.

commonClauses.topic: Required report content — the CIRCIA report template
commonClauses.obligationText: Reports must include: (a) identification of the covered entity, (b) description of the covered cyber incident or ransom payment, (c) date and time of detection and of the relevant determination, (d) affected information systems, networks, and devices, (e) impact (operational, financial, customer / supplier, mission, safety), (f) any vulnerabilities exploited, (g) suspected threat actor identity / TTPs, (h) security defences in place, (i) mitigation and recovery actions taken or planned, (j) contact details for the covered entity's reporting point of contact, (k) for ransom: payment amount, crypto address, threat-actor wallet, payment instructions.

commonClauses.topic: Third-party reporting — incident-response firms, insurance carriers, MSSP partners
commonClauses.obligationText: The NPRM permits but does not require a third party (incident-response firm, MSSP, insurance carrier) to submit a CIRCIA report on behalf of the covered entity. The covered entity remains legally responsible for the accuracy and completeness of the report and for any supplemental reports. Third-party arrangements must be documented in writing including the scope of authorisation, contact and credential exchange, and revocation pathway.

Covered entities must preserve data and records relevant to the reported incident for at least 2 years from the date of the most recent report relating to the incident. Preserved data includes: communications and notifications about the incident; system, network, and security logs from affected systems for a period covering at least the time immediately before through the time of remediation; analyses, reports, and other documentation produced by or for the entity related to the incident; copies of relevant indicators of compromise.

A supplemental report must be filed promptly when any of the following occurs: (a) the covered entity gains new or different substantial information about the incident, (b) the covered entity learns that the covered cyber incident is more or less severe than first reported, (c) the covered entity makes a ransom payment after the original report, (d) the covered entity completes a forensic analysis with new findings, (e) the covered entity concludes the incident and achieves full recovery (the final supplemental report).

commonClauses.topic: CIRCIA Agreement — sector-specific bridge to other federal reporting
commonClauses.obligationText: CISA may enter into a CIRCIA Agreement with another Federal agency that already requires covered cyber-incident reporting (NRC, FCC, FDA, FAA, FERC, SEC, EPA) so that a single report submitted to the partner agency is treated as satisfying both reporting obligations. Covered entities relying on a CIRCIA Agreement must verify the Agreement is in force, that the report meets the partner agency's requirements, and that the partner agency forwards to CISA within 24 hours.

Covered entities must maintain auditable, tamper-resistant records of the determination point ('reasonably believes covered cyber incident occurred'), the report submission point, the ransom-payment timestamp, and every supplemental-report submission. Such records support compliance demonstration to CISA on subpoena and form the basis for CIRCIA Agreement evidence sharing.

commonClauses.topic: CISA Services Portal — the canonical reporting channel
commonClauses.obligationText: Reports are submitted via the CISA Services Portal (https://services.cisa.gov) using web form, JSON API, or assisted submission. The covered entity's reporting point of contact must hold credentials, must have completed identity-proofing, and must be authorised to bind the entity. Out-of-band fallback (phone to the CISA 24/7 Operations Center) is permitted only during portal outage and must be followed by a portal-submitted report within 24 hours of portal restoration.

commonClauses.topic: Interim reporting (pre-Final Rule) — voluntary but CISA-encouraged
commonClauses.obligationText: Until the CIRCIA Final Rule is in force, CISA encourages covered entities to voluntarily report covered cyber-incidents and ransom payments through the same CISA Services Portal. Voluntary reports do not carry statutory enforcement consequences but do carry the CIRCIA liability protections and trigger CISA sector-coordination support.

commonClauses.topic: Protected information handling — CISA's safeguards on the report content
commonClauses.obligationText: CISA must handle CIRCIA reports under enhanced confidentiality: reports are exempt from FOIA; reports are not subject to state public-records requests; reports are subject to controlled access within CISA and sharing only with the minimum federal partners necessary for sector coordination, threat intelligence, and enforcement. Covered entities should mark sensitive elements (PII, customer data, trade secret) for additional handling.

CISA shares covered-cyber-incident reports with the relevant Sector Risk Management Agency (e.g. TSA for transportation, FERC for energy, EPA for water, HHS for healthcare, DoD for defense industrial base) for sector-coordination support. Covered entities should expect SRMA outreach following a covered-cyber-incident report and should pre-plan their SRMA liaison contact.

commonClauses.topic: Internal classification — distinguishing CIRCIA-reportable from sectoral-only
commonClauses.obligationText: Covered entities must implement an internal classification step in their incident-response workflow that distinguishes: (a) covered cyber incidents (CIRCIA 72-hour CISA reporting), (b) ransom payments (CIRCIA 24-hour CISA reporting), (c) sectoral-only incidents (e.g. EPA AWIA reporting only, not CIRCIA), (d) overlapping incidents (CIRCIA plus a sectoral framework). Classification must be timestamped and auditable to support enforcement defense.

commonClauses.topic: Third-party incident attribution — supply chain, MSSP, cloud
commonClauses.obligationText: A covered entity must report a CIRCIA-covered cyber incident even when the proximate cause is a compromise of a third-party cloud, MSSP, or supply-chain provider — if the impact on the covered entity meets the covered-incident threshold. The 72-hour clock starts when the covered entity reasonably believes the incident has occurred, regardless of when the third party itself detected the underlying compromise.

commonClauses.topic: OT / ICS / SCADA incidents — explicit in-scope under CIRCIA
commonClauses.obligationText: Covered cyber incidents include those affecting OT, ICS, SCADA, PLC, HMI, safety-instrumented systems, and other operational technology in any covered sector. CISA's pre-Final-Rule advisories on Iranian-affiliated water-sector PLC compromise (AA23-335A), CISA water-sector intrusion alerts, and Volt Typhoon prepositioning all illustrate the OT-incident scope. The 72-hour clock applies on the same terms.

commonClauses.topic: Board fiduciary awareness — pre-incident assignment of responsibility
commonClauses.obligationText: Covered entities should assign internal accountability for CIRCIA reporting at the board / executive level so that the 72-hour and 24-hour clocks are met without an in-incident debate over authority. This typically requires CEO / CFO / General Counsel pre-approval of the CISO's authority to file CIRCIA reports without further escalation, and an explicit fallback (Deputy CISO; on-call IR Commander) for out-of-hours incidents.

commonClauses.topic: SEC Form 8-K Item 1.05 alignment — materiality + CIRCIA
commonClauses.obligationText: For SEC registrants, the SEC Cybersecurity Disclosure Rule (effective December 2023) requires Form 8-K Item 1.05 disclosure of material cybersecurity incidents within 4 business days of materiality determination. CIRCIA and SEC reporting are independent: a covered entity may have a CIRCIA report due before the SEC materiality determination is complete; covered entities must process both pathways with appropriate counsel coordination.

commonClauses.topic: Annual tabletop — CIRCIA reporting workflow exercise
commonClauses.obligationText: Covered entities should exercise their CIRCIA reporting workflow at least annually as part of incident-response tabletop activity. The exercise must include: detection-to-determination timing; 72-hour and 24-hour clock simulation; portal credential health-check; SRMA liaison contact verification; cross-counsel coordination (SEC, sectoral, state); records-preservation activation. The exercise must be documented and retained as auditable evidence.

commonClauses.topic: CIRCIA-specific records retention — 2 years per NPRM
commonClauses.obligationText: Covered entities must retain CIRCIA reports, supplemental reports, and the data and records relevant to the reported incident for at least 2 years from the most recent report. The retained set must support CISA subpoena response within 72 hours; this implies an immutable, searchable, low-friction retrieval architecture spanning logs, communications, forensic artefacts, and indicator-of-compromise data.

pendingChanges.subject: CIRCIA Final Rule (CISA)
pendingChanges.status: The April 2024 NPRM is the proposed final rule; the Final Rule is expected in late 2025 / 2026 after CISA processes public comments. Once in force (likely 18 months after final rule publication), CIRCIA reporting becomes mandatory with civil enforcement authority.

pendingChanges.subject: Cyber Incident Reporting Council harmonisation
pendingChanges.status: The Cyber Incident Reporting Council (DHS-chaired) was established by CIRCIA Section 1752 to harmonise federal cyber-incident reporting; its September 2023 report proposes 24+ reporting harmonisation actions across federal agencies. Implementing actions roll out 2024-2026 and will refine the CIRCIA / SRMA / sectoral reporting interfaces.

frameworks.name: CLC/TS 50701:2021 — Railway applications - Cybersecurity (with IEC 63452 forward-alignment)

versions.grammarNotes: Clauses refer to the numbered sections of CLC/TS 50701:2021. 'c4' covers terms and definitions, 'c5' covers cybersecurity management, 'c6' covers risk assessment and treatment (zones, conduits, threat scenarios, vulnerability analysis), 'c7' covers cybersecurity requirements and security levels (SL-T target), 'c8' covers cybersecurity assurance (vulnerability handling, patching, incident response), 'c9' covers supply chain and procurement, 'c10' covers compliance with safety standards EN 50128 / EN 50129 / EN 50657, 'c11' covers operational cybersecurity (operator's continuous obligations), 'c12' covers maintenance cybersecurity. Annexes A-F provide informative guidance. CLC/TS 50701 is built on IEC 62443 zone-and-conduit modelling and inherits SL-1 through SL-4 security levels. The forward-alignment to IEC 63452 (Railway applications - Cybersecurity, under development at IEC TC 9) preserves the clause structure with refinements; pendingChanges tracks the IEC 63452 publication timeline.

commonClauses.topic: Cybersecurity management system (CSMS) — duty to establish
commonClauses.obligationText: The rail entity (infrastructure manager, railway undertaking, manufacturer, integrator, or maintenance supplier) must establish, document, implement, and continually improve a Cybersecurity Management System (CSMS) covering the lifecycle of the rail control / command / signalling / communications systems within its scope. The CSMS must define roles and responsibilities, processes, controls, and metrics, and must be integrated with the rail safety management system.

The rail entity must designate a Cybersecurity Manager (or equivalent role) accountable to executive management for the CSMS. The Cybersecurity Manager must coordinate with the Safety Manager required by EN 50126 / EN 50128 / EN 50129. Roles for cybersecurity risk owner, system integrator, supplier interface, and incident responder must be defined and competency-assured.

commonClauses.topic: Documented scope and asset inventory of the railway-system-under-consideration (SuC)
commonClauses.obligationText: The rail entity must define and document the System under Consideration (SuC) including all rail control, command, signalling, communications, traction, station-control, depot, and rolling-stock OT components within scope. The asset inventory must include version / firmware / configuration / connectivity / safety-classification metadata sufficient to support zone-and-conduit modelling under clause 6.

commonClauses.topic: Risk assessment — zones, conduits, and threat scenarios
commonClauses.obligationText: The rail entity must conduct a cybersecurity risk assessment of the SuC using IEC 62443-3-2 zone-and-conduit methodology adapted to rail. The assessment must identify zones (groups of assets with shared cybersecurity requirements), conduits (interconnections between zones), threat scenarios, vulnerabilities, and resulting risks. The risk-assessment outcomes must be documented and reviewed when the SuC or threat landscape changes materially.

The threat-scenario library must include railway-specific scenarios: ETCS / CBTC interference, signalling-track-circuit spoofing, point-machine command manipulation, ATO / ATP / ATC bypass, train-radio command-injection (GSM-R, FRMCS), rolling-stock cybersecurity attacks (HVAC, traction, door control, brake control), depot-network compromise affecting maintenance, and supplier-engineering remote-access compromise. Scenarios must be informed by ERA, ENISA, sector-CERTs (CERT-RAIL, RailCSIRT), and operator threat-intel.

commonClauses.topic: Risk treatment — controls aligned with safety-significant outcomes
commonClauses.obligationText: The rail entity must select cybersecurity controls to treat identified risks. Where a cybersecurity failure could degrade safety, the cybersecurity treatment must be coordinated with the safety case under EN 50126 / EN 50128 / EN 50129. Risk-acceptance decisions must be documented and signed by the cybersecurity risk owner; residual risks must be tracked.

Each zone identified in clause 6 must be assigned a Target Security Level (SL-T) on the IEC 62443 SL-1 through SL-4 scale. The SL-T must be commensurate with the consequences of a cybersecurity failure, with SL-T 3 or SL-T 4 typical for zones whose failure could degrade safety. SL-T determines the IEC 62443-3-3 system security requirements (SR) that must be met.

For each zone, the system must satisfy the IEC 62443-3-3 system security requirements (SR 1.1 — 7.6) appropriate to its SL-T. Categories include: identification and authentication control (IAC), use control (UC), system integrity (SI), data confidentiality (DC), restricted data flow (RDF), timely response to events (TRE), resource availability (RA). The rail integration must address these for signalling, comms, traction, station-control, and depot zones.

Components (PLCs, interlockings, axle counters, balises / Eurobalises, point machines, HMIs, on-board ETCS / ATP / ATC computers, train radios, traction-control inverters) must satisfy the IEC 62443-4-2 component security requirements (CR 1.1 — 7.6) commensurate with the SL-T of the zone in which they are deployed. Suppliers must produce evidence of CR compliance as part of the supply-chain artefact set (clause 9).

The rail entity must establish a vulnerability-handling process covering disclosure pathways (researcher, supplier, sector-CERT), triage against the SuC asset inventory, risk-based prioritisation, remediation planning including any safety-impact assessment under EN 50128 / EN 50129, and evidenced remediation closure. Disclosed vulnerabilities affecting safety-significant zones must be tracked with SLAs proportionate to risk.

commonClauses.topic: Patch management — coordinated with the safety case
commonClauses.obligationText: OT patch management must be performed under change control coordinated with the safety case under EN 50128 / EN 50129. Patches must be validated in a test environment, must not invalidate the safety case, and must follow the operator's railway-undertaking authorisation pathway (NSA / NSB acceptance where applicable under the EU Railway Interoperability Directive 2016/797). Emergency patches in response to active exploitation must follow a documented expedited pathway.

The rail entity must implement cybersecurity-incident detection covering OT zones (signalling, command-control, communications) and supporting IT zones (engineering workstations, depot networks, supplier remote-access endpoints). Detection must include OT-aware sensing (industrial protocol monitoring, anomaly detection on signalling-control traffic) and centralised correlation. Detection coverage must be evidenced against the threat-scenario library (clause 6).

commonClauses.topic: Cybersecurity incident response — coordinated with safety incident response
commonClauses.obligationText: The rail entity must establish an incident-response process for cybersecurity incidents affecting the SuC. The process must coordinate with the existing safety incident-response process (under EN 50126 and the rail entity's safety management system) so that a cybersecurity incident with safety implications triggers both procedures concurrently. Roles must be defined for IR Commander, Safety Liaison, NIS2 / NIS-equivalent regulatory reporting, and ERA / NSA / NSB reporting.

commonClauses.topic: Regulatory reporting bridge — NIS2 / national rail regulator / ERA
commonClauses.obligationText: Rail entities that fall within NIS2 scope (most rail infrastructure managers and large rail undertakings) must coordinate CLC/TS 50701 incident-response with NIS2 reporting (24-hour early-warning, 72-hour incident notification, 1-month final report). Where the rail entity is also subject to a national rail-cyber regulator (ANSSI for France's national rail OIVs, BSI for German rail KRITIS, NCSC for UK rail CNI), parallel reporting may apply.

The rail entity must impose cybersecurity obligations on suppliers and integrators including: SL-T-aligned component evidence (IEC 62443-4-2), Software Bill of Materials (SBOM), vulnerability-disclosure programme, secure-by-design and secure-development practice (IEC 62443-4-1), patch and maintenance commitments, decommissioning, and Right to Audit. Obligations must flow down to sub-suppliers via the prime supplier's contract.

commonClauses.topic: Supplier-engineering remote access — controlled and observed
commonClauses.obligationText: Supplier and integrator remote access into the SuC for commissioning, maintenance, and incident-response must be controlled (named credentials, MFA, time-boxed, scoped), observed (full session recording on PAM, jump host, or supplier-side proxy), and auditable. Persistent supplier VPN is prohibited; just-in-time access tied to a ticket is the required pattern.

commonClauses.topic: Procurement — cybersecurity evaluation of supplier proposals
commonClauses.obligationText: Procurement of rail control, command, signalling, communications, and traction systems must include cybersecurity evaluation of supplier proposals: component SL-C evidence; SBOM disclosure; vulnerability handling commitments; reference architecture review; integration risk-assessment alignment. Cybersecurity scoring must be a material part of award decisions, not a check-box at the end.

commonClauses.topic: Coordination with safety standards (EN 50126 / EN 50128 / EN 50129 / EN 50657)
commonClauses.obligationText: Cybersecurity activities must coordinate with the rail safety standards: EN 50126 (RAM safety lifecycle), EN 50128 (software for railway control / protection systems), EN 50129 (electronic systems for signalling), EN 50657 (on-board software). A change with cybersecurity implications must trigger a safety-case impact review; a change to satisfy a cybersecurity requirement that alters safety-relevant behaviour must be re-validated for safety.

Where a cybersecurity risk has a credible pathway to a safety hazard, the residual risk must be jointly evaluated by the cybersecurity risk owner and the safety manager, and must be jointly accepted. Risk acceptance must reference both the safety integrity level (SIL) and the target security level (SL-T) of the affected zone, and the joint assessment must be retained for the lifetime of the SuC plus regulator-required retention.

The rail operator (infrastructure manager or railway undertaking, depending on which is responsible for the deployed SuC) must execute continuous cybersecurity activities: monitoring, vulnerability handling, patching, access governance, training, supplier-relationship management, incident response, and review of risk-assessment outputs. Continuous activities must be tracked against KPIs and reviewed by the Cybersecurity Manager on a defined cadence.

commonClauses.topic: Awareness and competence — railway-specific cybersecurity training
commonClauses.obligationText: Personnel with cybersecurity responsibilities (Cybersecurity Manager, IR Commander, OT-aware analyst, signalling engineer, depot maintainer, train driver / dispatcher with operational cyber awareness) must have railway-specific cybersecurity competence appropriate to their role. Training programmes must cover OT-specific physical-tampering recognition, signalling-control-command awareness, supplier-engineering-remote-access etiquette, and incident-reporting workflow.

commonClauses.topic: Continuous risk-assessment refresh — re-trigger on material change
commonClauses.obligationText: The risk assessment (clause 6) must be refreshed on a defined cadence (at minimum every 3 years for production SuCs, more frequently for high-impact systems) and re-triggered on material change to the SuC, on new safety findings, on new sector-CERT advisories materially affecting the threat-scenario library, and after any cybersecurity incident with safety implications.

Cybersecurity controls must extend through maintenance activities including depot software-update workflow, on-board diagnostic-port access, balise / Eurobalise programming, supplier-laptop hygiene, and removable-media handling. Maintenance personnel must be trained on the cybersecurity expectations of their roles and must use only authorised, hardened, and inventoried laptops / tools / USB devices.

Maintenance laptops with physical access to the SuC (signalling-cabin laptop, on-board diagnostic laptop, balise-programming laptop) must satisfy a cybersecurity baseline: full-disk encryption, MDM enrolment, EDR with on-network and off-network signalling, removable-media policy, application-allow-listing, regular patching, and tracked through the rail entity's CMDB. Laptops without baseline compliance must not be connected to the SuC.

commonClauses.topic: Decommissioning cybersecurity — end-of-life data and components
commonClauses.obligationText: Decommissioning of SuC components must address cybersecurity: configuration export and analysis for residual signalling-knowledge value; cryptographic-key destruction; removal of authorised-credentials from interconnected systems; decommissioning audit trail. Salvage and resale of decommissioned components require sanitisation to prevent leak of signalling configuration.

commonClauses.topic: Threat-actor types relevant to railway cybersecurity
commonClauses.obligationText: Annex A enumerates threat-actor types relevant to railway cybersecurity: nation-state targeting national-rail strategic infrastructure (Volt Typhoon-style prepositioning), hacktivist groups protesting transport policy or geopolitics, criminal ransomware aimed at extortion (Vy / NS / DB / SNCF / FS / Renfe / OBB historical events), insider threats (disgruntled engineer with signalling-system knowledge), supply-chain-compromise actor reaching the rail entity through a supplier. Each actor type implies different controls and detection.

Annex D presents reference architectures for typical rail SuCs: mainline signalling (interlocking, RBC, ETCS Level 2, GSM-R / FRMCS), urban metro CBTC, station-control, depot-network, rolling-stock on-board signalling (ETCS on-board, ATP, ATO, ATC, comfort systems). Each reference architecture identifies typical zones, their SL-T, and the conduits between them — used as the starting point for clause-6 risk assessment.

The rail operator must perform a CLC/TS 50701 self-assessment at least annually against the documented CSMS, evidencing role-coverage, risk-assessment currency, SL-T coverage, vulnerability-handling SLA performance, training completion, supplier-compliance status, and incident-response readiness. Self-assessment results must be reviewed by executive management and any findings tracked to closure. Where the rail entity is subject to NSA / NSB or national-rail-cyber-regulator oversight (ANSSI, BSI, NCSC), the self-assessment evidence must be producible on request.

pendingChanges.subject: IEC 63452 (Railway applications - Cybersecurity, IEC TC 9) — international successor
pendingChanges.status: IEC TC 9 is developing IEC 63452 as the international successor to CLC/TS 50701. Committee Draft (CD) circulated 2023; Committee Draft for Vote (CDV) expected 2025; final IEC 63452 publication targeted 2026. CLC/TS 50701 is expected to remain in use during the transition; rail entities should track IEC 63452 to anticipate clause adjustments.

pendingChanges.subject: EU Cyber Resilience Act (CRA) intersection with railway components
pendingChanges.status: The EU Cyber Resilience Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/2847, entry into force 11 December 2024, main obligations apply from 11 December 2027) applies to digital elements placed on the EU market — including many railway components.
