Railway suppliers must align CLC/TS 50701 c7 and c9 obligations with CRA cybersecurity by design requirements; rail entities (as operators) must update procurement specifications to require CRA-conformant components from December 2027.

frameworks.name: TSA Surface Cybersecurity Security Directives (SD-Pipeline-2021-01/02 + amendments, SD-1580/82-2022-01 freight + passenger rail, SD-1582-21 cyber TSA OT cybersecurity)

versions.grammarNotes: TSA Surface Security Directives are a layered regime. The Pipeline series (SD-Pipeline-2021-01, -02, and -02A through -02G amendments) covers liquid and natural gas pipelines. The Rail series (SD-1580/82-2022-01 for freight rail; SD-1582-21-01 / SD-1582-2022-01 for passenger rail) covers Class I freight railroads and major passenger railroads (Amtrak + PTC operators). All SDs share a common contractual structure: (s2) Reporting cybersecurity incidents to CISA, (s3) Designating a Cybersecurity Coordinator, (s4) Developing and implementing a Cybersecurity Incident Response Plan (CIRP) tested annually, (s5) Conducting a Cybersecurity Assessment (CSA) including CSAS — Cybersecurity Architecture Assessment Submission. Pipeline-specific s6 covers Cybersecurity Implementation Plan (CIP) with control families (cf-1 through cf-10). The cyber-SD-Surface regime is reissued / amended approximately annually; the 'consolidated-pipeline-rail' version captures the in-force baseline as of June 2024 plus the 2024-07 reauthorisation.

commonClauses.topic: Pipeline — Cybersecurity Incident Reporting to CISA within 24 hours
commonClauses.obligationText: TSA-regulated pipeline owner/operators (Hazardous Liquids and Natural Gas pipelines designated as 'critical' under 49 CFR 1572.5) must report cybersecurity incidents to CISA within 24 hours of discovery. Reportable events include: (a) unauthorized access to an Information or Operational Technology (IT/OT) system; (b) discovery of malicious software on an IT/OT system; (c) activity resulting in a denial of service to any IT/OT system; (d) physical attack against the cyber infrastructure; (e) ransomware demand against the system.

Each TSA-regulated pipeline must designate a Cybersecurity Coordinator (CC) and an Alternate CC who are US citizens and eligible to handle Sensitive Security Information (SSI). The CC must be available 24×7 to coordinate cybersecurity activities with TSA, CISA, and the FBI. The CC's name, title, phone number, and email must be reported to TSA within 7 days of designation or change.

commonClauses.topic: Pipeline — Cybersecurity Implementation Plan (CIP) — control family 1 (network segmentation)
commonClauses.obligationText: The Cybersecurity Implementation Plan (CIP) — required by SD-Pipeline-2021-02 and detailed in -02C — must establish and maintain network segmentation policies and controls that separate Operational Technology (OT) systems from Information Technology (IT) systems, including a 'zero trust' style architecture or compensating controls that prevent or limit unauthorized communications between IT and OT. Segmentation must be implemented at the technology (firewall, DMZ, data-diode) and process (change-control approval) layers.

Pipeline CIP must implement strong access control on critical cyber systems: identification and authentication (preferring MFA for privileged access into OT), least privilege, separation of duties, and quarterly access-review of privileged accounts. Service accounts must be inventoried with documented purpose and ownership. Vendor remote access must be controlled and observed with full session recording.

commonClauses.topic: Pipeline — CIP control family 3 (continuous monitoring and detection)
commonClauses.obligationText: Pipeline CIP must implement continuous monitoring and detection of OT and IT systems for cybersecurity events. Monitoring must include detection of (a) anomalous network traffic crossing the IT/OT boundary, (b) unauthorized changes to OT system configuration, (c) malicious software, (d) ransomware encryption activity, and (e) other indicators of compromise relevant to the threat-actor profile (including Volt Typhoon and APT prepositioning). Detection must produce timely, actionable alerts.

commonClauses.topic: Pipeline — CIP control family 4 (timely patching with OT considerations)
commonClauses.obligationText: Pipeline CIP must include a risk-based patch management programme for IT and OT. Critical security patches must be applied within timeframes commensurate with the risk of the unpatched vulnerability. For OT, where patching could affect safety / availability, compensating controls (network isolation, allow-listing) may be applied with documented operational rationale and a managed-risk decision. Annual reviews of compensating-control effectiveness are required.

commonClauses.topic: Pipeline — Cybersecurity Incident Response Plan (CIRP) with annual exercise
commonClauses.obligationText: Each TSA-regulated pipeline must develop, maintain, and exercise annually a Cybersecurity Incident Response Plan (CIRP) addressing: (a) procedures for identifying and reporting cybersecurity incidents, (b) procedures for containing and eradicating compromises while preserving the ability to provide gas / oil service, (c) procedures for restoring affected IT and OT systems, (d) communication procedures with TSA, CISA, FBI, and (where applicable) PHMSA, (e) post-incident lessons-learned. The plan must be exercised at least annually with documented after-action and corrective-action tracking.

commonClauses.topic: Pipeline — Cybersecurity Assessment (CSA) including CSAS / CAA
commonClauses.obligationText: Each TSA-regulated pipeline must complete an annual Cybersecurity Assessment of the critical cyber systems against the CIP control families. The assessment must include a Cybersecurity Architecture Assessment Submission (CSAS) — a documented description of the IT/OT architecture, zone-and-conduit / segmentation model, and external connectivity. CSA results must be reported to TSA on the prescribed cadence and made available for TSA / CISA inspection.

commonClauses.topic: Freight Rail — Cybersecurity Incident Reporting to CISA within 24 hours
commonClauses.obligationText: TSA-regulated freight railroads (Class I freight railroads identified under 49 CFR 1580.101 and additional designated entities) must report cybersecurity incidents to CISA within 24 hours of discovery. Reportable categories mirror the Pipeline SD: unauthorized access, malicious software, denial of service, ransomware demand, and physical attack against cyber infrastructure. Reports must use the TSA-designated reporting mechanism (currently CISA Services Portal with TSA notice copy).

Each TSA-regulated freight railroad must designate a Cybersecurity Coordinator (CC) and Alternate, both US citizens with SSI clearance, available 24×7. Identity / contact information must be reported to TSA within 7 days of designation or change.

commonClauses.topic: Freight Rail — Cybersecurity Incident Response Plan (CIRP) with annual exercise
commonClauses.obligationText: Each TSA-regulated freight railroad must develop, maintain, and exercise annually a Cybersecurity Incident Response Plan covering OT (signalling, PTC, dispatch) and IT systems. The plan must address rail-specific scenarios including PTC degradation, dispatch-system unavailability, and grade-crossing-warning-system compromise. Annual exercise + after-action documentation required.

Each TSA-regulated freight railroad must perform a Cybersecurity Vulnerability Assessment of critical cyber systems on an annual cadence. The assessment must use a recognized methodology (NIST CSF, NIST 800-53, NIST 800-82) and document an action plan with assigned owners and target remediation dates.

commonClauses.topic: Passenger Rail — Cybersecurity Incident Reporting to CISA within 24 hours
commonClauses.obligationText: TSA-regulated passenger rail entities (Amtrak and PTC-operating commuter / intercity operators identified under 49 CFR 1582) must report cybersecurity incidents to CISA within 24 hours of discovery using the prescribed mechanism. Reportable categories include unauthorized access, malicious software, denial of service, ransomware demand, and physical attack against cyber infrastructure that could disrupt rail operations or endanger passengers / employees.

Each TSA-regulated passenger rail entity must designate a Cybersecurity Coordinator + Alternate (US citizens, SSI cleared, 24×7 reachable) and report identity / contact to TSA within 7 days of designation or change.

commonClauses.topic: Passenger Rail — Cybersecurity Incident Response Plan (CIRP) with annual exercise
commonClauses.obligationText: Each TSA-regulated passenger rail entity must develop, maintain, and exercise annually a Cybersecurity Incident Response Plan covering OT (PTC, signalling, dispatch, traction power) and IT systems. Plan must address rail-specific scenarios including PTC degradation, dispatch-system compromise, ticketing / fare-collection compromise, and onboard-system compromise. Annual exercise + after-action required.

The Passenger Rail CIP must establish and maintain network segmentation policies separating OT (PTC back-office, signalling, dispatch) from IT (corporate, ticketing). Segmentation must be implemented at the technology layer (firewalls, DMZs, data-diodes where applicable) and the process layer (change-control approval for IT/OT boundary changes). Architecture must support 'no implicit trust between zones' principle.

commonClauses.topic: Passenger Rail — control family 2 (access control + MFA for OT)
commonClauses.obligationText: Passenger Rail CIP must implement strong access control: MFA for privileged accounts (preferred for routine access into OT), least privilege, quarterly access-review of privileged accounts, inventoried service accounts with documented purpose and ownership, and controlled / observed vendor remote access with full session recording.

commonClauses.topic: Passenger Rail — control family 3 (continuous monitoring + detection)
commonClauses.obligationText: Passenger Rail CIP must implement continuous monitoring and detection of OT and IT systems for cybersecurity events. Detection must include anomalous traffic at the IT/OT boundary, unauthorized changes to OT configuration, malicious software, ransomware indicators, and OT-specific indicators (PTC anomalies, signalling-control anomalies). Detection must produce actionable alerts within timeframes that support 24-hour CISA reporting.

commonClauses.topic: Passenger Rail — control family 4 (timely patching with OT considerations)
commonClauses.obligationText: Passenger Rail CIP must include a risk-based patch management programme for IT and OT. Critical patches must be applied within timeframes commensurate with risk. For OT (PTC back-office, signalling, dispatch), where patching could affect safety / availability, compensating controls (allow-listing, network isolation) may be applied with documented operational rationale.

commonClauses.topic: Passenger Rail — control family 5 (cybersecurity assessment + CSAS)
commonClauses.obligationText: Each TSA-regulated passenger rail entity must complete an annual Cybersecurity Assessment of critical cyber systems against the CIP control families, including a Cybersecurity Architecture Assessment Submission (CSAS) describing IT/OT architecture, segmentation model, and external connectivity. Results reportable to TSA on prescribed cadence.

commonClauses.topic: Surface — Authorized Compliance Official designation (where applicable)
commonClauses.obligationText: Where the TSA Surface regime requires designation of an Authorized Compliance Official (ACO) — typically for high-criticality designations and certain dual-modal (pipeline + rail) entities — the entity must designate, train, and notify TSA of the ACO. ACO has authority to sign CSAS, CIP attestations, and represent the entity in TSA inspections.

commonClauses.topic: Pipeline — Annual CIP attestation and corrective-action submission
commonClauses.obligationText: TSA-regulated pipelines must submit annually an attestation that the CIP remains accurate and reflects the current cyber posture, accompanied by a corrective-action plan addressing any gaps. Material control failures discovered during the year must be documented and the CIP / corrective-action plan updated to reflect remediation timelines.

Pipeline CIP must address supply chain cybersecurity: third-party access controls; supplier evidence of cyber-control implementation; SBOM where available for critical IT/OT components; vendor remote-access controls (session recording, time-bounded access, named credentials); and security-of-services-supply-chain (managed-service-provider obligations).

Pipeline CIP must include configuration management for critical OT systems: documented baseline configurations; change-control approval before deployment; verification that deployed configuration matches approved baseline; periodic configuration audit; integrity monitoring of OT firmware / software / configuration where technically feasible.

commonClauses.topic: Freight Rail — Annual CIP attestation and corrective-action submission
commonClauses.obligationText: Freight rail entities must submit annual attestation that the CIRP / CSA cycle is current and corrective actions are tracked to closure. Material control failures must be documented and corrective-action plan updated.

commonClauses.topic: Pipeline — Reporting cyber incidents that affect physical operations
commonClauses.obligationText: Cybersecurity incidents that result in or have the potential to result in operational impact (pipeline shut-down, pressure-loss, leak-detection-system compromise) must be reported to TSA within 12 hours (faster than the 24-hour cyber-incident reporting under s2) with a parallel safety-incident notification to PHMSA where the event has a physical-safety dimension.

Pipeline CIP must include recovery and resilience testing of critical cyber systems on an annual cadence: backup integrity testing, restore-from-backup exercises, OT system restoration to a known-good state, and validated communications continuity during a cyber incident.

commonClauses.topic: Passenger Rail — Configuration management for OT
commonClauses.obligationText: Passenger Rail CIP must include configuration management for critical OT systems (PTC, signalling, dispatch): documented baseline configurations, change-control approval, verification that deployed configuration matches approved baseline, periodic configuration audit, and integrity monitoring where technically feasible.

pendingChanges.subject: TSA Surface Cybersecurity Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM) for Surface Transportation Pipeline + Rail
pendingChanges.status: TSA published NPRM (89 FR 88488, November 7, 2024) proposing to codify the Surface Transportation Cybersecurity requirements (formerly issued as Security Directives) into permanent regulation at 49 CFR Parts 1580, 1582, 1584, and 1586. Public comment closes February 5, 2025; final rule expected late 2025 / early 2026. The catalogue tracks the Surface SD regime as the in-force baseline pending final rule.

frameworks.name: Cybersecurity Act 2018 (Singapore) and the Cybersecurity (Critical Information Infrastructure) Regulations 2018

versions.grammarNotes: The Cybersecurity Act 2018 (Singapore) establishes the Cyber Security Agency of Singapore (CSA), creates the framework for designating and regulating Critical Information Infrastructure (CII), and grants the Commissioner of Cybersecurity statutory powers. Sections used in catalogue mappings: s7 (CII designation), s10 (Code of Practice issuance), s14 (Owner duties — incident reporting), s15 (Owner duties — audit + risk assessment), s16 (Cybersecurity exercises), s19 (Investigation powers). The Cybersecurity (Critical Information Infrastructure) Regulations 2018 (SG-CII-Reg) further specify reporting forms and timelines. The CSA Code of Practice (COP) — SG-CSA-COC-* — sets implementation detail. Amendments through 2024 strengthen the SOC-of-CSA notification regime and expand CII categories. CII is regulated across 11 sectors: Banking & Finance, Energy, Government, Healthcare, Info-Communications, Land Transport, Maritime, Media, Security & Emergency Services, Water, and Aviation.

commonClauses.topic: CII designation — Commissioner's power and Owner's duties on designation
commonClauses.obligationText: The Commissioner of Cybersecurity may designate a computer or computer system as a Critical Information Infrastructure (CII) where its loss / compromise would have a debilitating effect on the availability of an essential service in Singapore. The CII Owner must, upon designation, acknowledge in writing and comply with all duties under Part 3 of the Act and the CII Regulations 2018.

commonClauses.topic: Code of Practice — binding implementation guidance
commonClauses.obligationText: The Commissioner may issue or approve a Code of Practice (COP) or Standard of Performance specifying cybersecurity measures for CII Owners. The current binding COP is the CSA Code of Practice for Critical Information Infrastructure (most recent edition: COP 2022, Edition 1, gazetted under s10). CII Owners must comply with the COP within the prescribed timeframes.

commonClauses.topic: CII Owner — reporting prescribed cybersecurity incidents to Commissioner
commonClauses.obligationText: A CII Owner must notify the Commissioner of any prescribed cybersecurity incident in relation to the CII within the prescribed period. Per CII Regulation 5, a 'prescribed cybersecurity incident' is reportable within 2 hours of awareness via the prescribed form (CSA Form A), with a written report within 14 days. Prescribed incidents include unauthorised access, malicious software, denial of service, and any incident with potential disruption to essential service delivery.

commonClauses.topic: CII Owner — reporting material change in CII
commonClauses.obligationText: A CII Owner must notify the Commissioner of any material change in the design, configuration, security, operation, or ownership of the CII within 30 days of the change. 'Material' includes changes in architecture, hosting location, ownership, primary contractor / managed-service-provider, or other elements affecting cybersecurity risk.

A CII Owner must conduct or arrange a cybersecurity audit of the CII at least once every 2 years (annually in higher-criticality sectors per the COP), and submit the audit report to the Commissioner. The audit must be conducted by an auditor approved by the Commissioner (CSA-approved auditor pool) and must cover compliance with the Act, Regulations, and applicable COP / Standards of Performance.

A CII Owner must conduct a cybersecurity risk assessment of the CII at least once every year, addressing technological, organisational, supply-chain, and threat-landscape risks. Results must be reported to the Commissioner in the prescribed form (CSA Form B). Risk-treatment decisions and residual-risk acceptance must be documented and approved at the senior-management level.

commonClauses.topic: CII Owner — participation in cybersecurity exercises
commonClauses.obligationText: A CII Owner must participate in cybersecurity exercises as directed by the Commissioner, including the biennial 'Exercise Cyber Star' sector-wide exercise coordinated by CSA. Participation includes scenario play, after-action reporting, and corrective-action tracking. Refusal or material failure to participate is an offence under the Act.

commonClauses.topic: Commissioner's investigation powers and Owner's cooperation duty
commonClauses.obligationText: The Commissioner has statutory powers to investigate cybersecurity incidents and threats including the power to require provision of information, technical assistance, access to systems, and forensic-evidence preservation. CII Owners must cooperate with investigations, designate a Cybersecurity Officer as the Commissioner's point of contact, and produce evidence (logs, configurations, incident artefacts) on demand.

commonClauses.topic: CII Owner — designation of Cybersecurity Officer + Alternate
commonClauses.obligationText: The CII Owner must designate a Cybersecurity Officer and an Alternate Cybersecurity Officer who together provide 24×7 cover for cybersecurity matters, are reachable by the Commissioner / CSA, and are authorised to make decisions / commitments on cybersecurity. Identity / contact information must be filed with CSA and updated within 7 days of change.

commonClauses.topic: CII Owner — prescribed cybersecurity incident reporting (2-hour rule)
commonClauses.obligationText: A CII Owner must report a 'prescribed cybersecurity incident' to the Commissioner within 2 hours of becoming aware of the incident (initial notification by phone + CSA Form A submission), followed by a written supplementary report within 14 days. Prescribed incidents are listed in the COP and Reg 5 and include unauthorized access, malicious code, denial of service, ransomware demand, and incidents with potential disruption to essential service.

Per the CSA COP, the CII Owner must maintain a comprehensive asset inventory of all systems and components within the CII boundary, including version / firmware / configuration metadata, criticality classification, ownership, and interconnections. Inventory must be updated within 14 days of material change.

commonClauses.topic: COP — identity, access, MFA, and privileged-account management
commonClauses.obligationText: Per the COP, access to the CII must be controlled with strong identification + authentication; MFA must be enforced for privileged accounts and for remote access from outside the CII trust boundary; privileged accounts must be inventoried, separated from regular accounts, used only when needed, and audited quarterly. Service / system accounts must be inventoried with documented purpose and ownership.

commonClauses.topic: COP — continuous monitoring and SOC-of-CSA notification
commonClauses.obligationText: The CII Owner must implement continuous monitoring producing security-event telemetry to a SOC capability (in-house or contracted). Per the SOC-of-CSA pathway (introduced under the 2024 amendments), aggregated indicators are flowable to CSA's national-level monitoring under defined privacy-respecting protocols. Material incidents trigger the 2-hour reporting clock under SG-CII-Reg-5.

commonClauses.topic: COP — third-party cybersecurity and managed-service obligations
commonClauses.obligationText: The COP requires CII Owners to manage third-party cybersecurity risks: pre-engagement risk assessment of vendors; contractual cybersecurity obligations including breach notification within 24 hours of vendor detection; supplier-side audit rights; SBOM / patch commitment for suppliers of critical IT/OT components; vendor remote-access controls including session recording.

commonClauses.topic: COP — Business continuity and disaster recovery for the CII
commonClauses.obligationText: The COP requires a Business Continuity Plan (BCP) and Disaster Recovery Plan (DRP) for the CII covering cyber-driven disruption scenarios. Plans must be exercised at least annually, must include cyber-incident-specific playbooks, must address restoration of essential service within sector-defined Recovery Time Objectives, and must be reported to the Commissioner on the prescribed cadence.

pendingChanges.subject: Cybersecurity (Amendment) Act 2024 implementation
pendingChanges.status: The Cybersecurity (Amendment) Act 2024 (passed 7 May 2024; gradual entry into force 2024-2025) expands CII to include 'Systems of Temporary Cybersecurity Concern' (STCC) and 'Major FSI' (Foundational Service Infrastructure) categories; strengthens incident-reporting and audit obligations; expands SOC-of-CSA participation; and increases penalties. CII Owners should track the amendment's transitional schedule and update internal compliance accordingly.

frameworks.name: Loi de programmation militaire (LPM) — French Operators of Vital Importance (OIV) cybersecurity regime + ANSSI implementing decrees

versions.grammarNotes: The LPM (Loi de programmation militaire) Article 22 (2013) established the French OIV (Opérateur d'Importance Vitale) cybersecurity regime, codified into the Code de la défense (articles L.1332-6-1 et seq.). Implementation is delegated to ANSSI (Agence nationale de la sécurité des systèmes d'information) via four key decrees: Décret 2015-350 (designation of SIIV — Systèmes d'Information d'Importance Vitale within OIVs); Décret 2015-351 (cybersecurity rules applicable to SIIV); Arrêté ANSSI 2016 (technical rules: 20 rules organised into 5 themes — Governance / Protection / Defence / Resilience / Identification of incidents); plus the LPM 2018 update extending OIV-equivalent regime to certain operators of essential services. OIV designations are confidential; the regime applies to approximately 270 organisations across 12 sectors (Energy, Water, Transport, Health, Banking & Finance, Telecoms, Food, Industry, Civil Activity of the State, Military Activity of the State, Aerospace, etc.). The LPM regime now overlaps with NIS2 (transposed in France by Loi 2024-364) — operators may be both OIV under LPM and 'entité essentielle' under NIS2, with LPM rules generally taking precedence where they are stricter.

commonClauses.topic: OIV designation — operator-of-vital-importance status and SIIV identification
commonClauses.obligationText: Article 22 of the LPM (codified at Code de la défense L.1332-6-1) requires designated Operators of Vital Importance (OIV) to identify their Systèmes d'Information d'Importance Vitale (SIIV) — information systems essential to delivery of vital activities — and apply ANSSI-prescribed cybersecurity rules. OIV designation is by Prime Minister decree (confidential); SIIV identification is the OIV's responsibility, validated by ANSSI.

commonClauses.topic: Décret 2015-351 — applicability of the 20 ANSSI cybersecurity rules to SIIV
commonClauses.obligationText: Décret 2015-351 du 27 mars 2015 specifies that the 20 cybersecurity rules issued by ANSSI (via Arrêté) apply to every SIIV. The rules are organised in 5 themes: 1. Governance (4 rules), 2. Protection (4 rules), 3. Defence (4 rules), 4. Resilience (3 rules), 5. Identification of incidents (5 rules). Compliance is mandatory and subject to ANSSI inspection.

commonClauses.topic: Governance rule 1 — Cybersecurity Officer (RSSI) for the OIV
commonClauses.obligationText: Each OIV must designate a Cybersecurity Officer (Responsable de la sécurité des systèmes d'information, RSSI) accountable for the SIIV-protection programme. The RSSI must have direct access to the OIV's executive management, be empowered to make and enforce cybersecurity decisions on SIIV, and be ANSSI's point of contact. Identity must be notified to ANSSI within 7 days of designation or change.

commonClauses.topic: Governance rule 2 — SIIV mapping and asset inventory
commonClauses.obligationText: The OIV must maintain a documented, accurate, and current mapping of every SIIV: scope, boundaries, components (hardware, software, firmware, network), interconnections with other SIIVs and non-SIIV systems, third-party access points, and dependencies. The mapping must be produced for ANSSI on request and updated within prescribed periods on material change.

commonClauses.topic: Protection rule — strong identity and access control + MFA
commonClauses.obligationText: Access to a SIIV must use strong authentication (preferring MFA) for privileged accounts and for any access from outside the SIIV trust boundary. Account management must enforce least privilege, periodic review (at least quarterly for privileged), separation of administrative and operational accounts, and prohibition of shared / generic accounts for accountability-critical functions.

commonClauses.topic: Defence rule — cybersecurity event detection + SOC capability
commonClauses.obligationText: Each OIV must establish a cybersecurity event detection capability for its SIIV (Security Operations Centre, in-house or qualified PDIS-supplier-operated). The detection capability must produce indicators of compromise actionable within timeframes that support the LPM 24-hour reporting clock and produce evidence usable by ANSSI investigators (CERT-FR).

commonClauses.topic: Defence rule — PDIS qualification for detection providers
commonClauses.obligationText: Where the OIV outsources its detection capability (Prestataire de Détection d'Incidents de Sécurité, PDIS), the provider must be ANSSI-qualified PDIS. The OIV remains accountable; contracts with PDIS providers must contemplate ANSSI inspection, evidence retention, and timely escalation.

commonClauses.topic: Identification-of-incidents rule — incident reporting to ANSSI
commonClauses.obligationText: Each OIV must report to ANSSI (CERT-FR) any cybersecurity incident affecting a SIIV without undue delay and, in any event, within prescribed timeframes (typically 24 hours of detection for material incidents; immediate notification for severe incidents). Reports must use the prescribed ANSSI form / portal and include initial scoping, indicators of compromise, and provisional impact assessment, with updates as the response progresses.

pendingChanges.subject: NIS2 transposition into French law via Loi 2024-364 and its interaction with the LPM OIV regime
pendingChanges.status: France transposed NIS2 (Directive (EU) 2022/2555) via Loi 2024-364 du 22 avril 2024 (in force progressively through 2025). Many OIVs are also 'entités essentielles' under NIS2; ANSSI guidance confirms that where LPM rules are stricter, they prevail; where NIS2 obligations exceed LPM (e.g. management-body cyber-training accountability), they apply. OIVs should track the unified ANSSI guidance for NIS2 + LPM compliance.

pendingChanges.subject: LPM 2024-2030 update — extension of OIV-equivalent regime to additional categories
pendingChanges.status: The LPM 2024-2030 (Loi 2023-703) includes provisions for incremental extension of cybersecurity obligations to operators not formally designated OIV but supporting critical national functions (notably in space, AI infrastructure, and supply-chain orchestrators). ANSSI implementing decrees expected progressively 2024-2026.

frameworks.name: IMO Resolution MSC.428(98) — Maritime Cyber Risk Management in Safety Management Systems, plus MSC-FAL.1/Circ.3 Rev.2 Guidelines and IACS UR E26 / E27 cyber-resilience unified requirements
aliases: BIMCO Guidelines on Cyber Security Onboard Ships

versions.grammarNotes: IMO Resolution MSC.428(98) was adopted by the Maritime Safety Committee on 16 June 2017 and entered into operational effect through the safety-management-system (SMS) verification cycle on 1 January 2021. The resolution mandates that an approved SMS — as required under the International Safety Management (ISM) Code (IMO Resolution A.741(18)) — must take into account cyber risk management no later than the first annual verification of the company's Document of Compliance (DoC) on or after 1 January 2021. Implementation detail is in IMO MSC-FAL.1/Circ.3 'Guidelines on maritime cyber risk management' (originally 5 July 2017; latest revision Rev.2 June 2022), which adopts the NIST CSF five-function model (Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover) and inventories cyber-vulnerable shipboard systems (§3.1 — bridge, propulsion + machinery + power, cargo management, communications, passenger servicing, administrative + crew welfare, access control). Equipment-level and ship-level cyber-resilience were further codified by the International Association of Classification Societies (IACS) via Unified Requirements UR E26 'Cyber resilience of ships' (ship-newbuild design) and UR E27 'Cyber resilience of on-board systems and equipment' (type-approval of OT equipment), both adopted April 2022 and applicable to ships contracted for newbuild construction on or after 1 July 2024. The BIMCO 'Guidelines on Cyber Security Onboard Ships' (v4 June 2020, v5 expected 2024) is the industry-standard implementation reference and is widely accepted by flag administrations, ROs (classification societies — DNV, ABS, Lloyd's, BV, KR, NK, RINA, CCS, IRS), and port-State control regimes (Paris MoU, Tokyo MoU, USCG) as evidence of 'appropriate cyber risk management.' Catalogue clause IDs use 'IMO-MSC-428-98-pN' for the four operative paragraphs of the resolution, 'IMO-MSC-FAL-Circ-3-sX-Y' for the sections of the guidelines, 'IACS-UR-E2[67]-rN' for the unified-requirements clauses, 'IMO-ISM-Code-sX-Y' for the underlying ISM Code, and 'BIMCO-Cyber-<topic>' for BIMCO Guidelines best-practice references.

commonClauses.topic: Affirmation — an approved safety management system shall take cyber risk into account
commonClauses.obligationText: Operative paragraph 1 of IMO Resolution MSC.428(98) affirms that an approved safety management system (SMS) — as required under the International Safety Management (ISM) Code — should take into account cyber risk management in accordance with the objectives and functional requirements of the ISM Code. Cyber risk is therefore a first-class element of the SMS rather than a separate parallel programme, and any non-conformity in cyber-risk management can render the SMS non-conforming.

commonClauses.topic: Annual DoC verification clock — cyber risks addressed no later than first DoC verification after 1 January 2021
commonClauses.obligationText: Operative paragraph 2 encourages flag administrations to ensure that cyber risks are appropriately addressed in safety management systems no later than the first annual verification of the company's Document of Compliance (DoC) after 1 January 2021. In practice, flag administrations and recognised organisations (classification societies acting on behalf of flag State) treat absence of a cyber-risk-managed SMS as a major non-conformity at DoC verification, with the consequence that the DoC may be withheld, conditioned, or withdrawn and the ship may be detained under port-State control under the Paris / Tokyo MoU or USCG regimes.

commonClauses.topic: Reference to MSC-FAL.1/Circ.3 — high-level recommendations on maritime cyber risk management
commonClauses.obligationText: Operative paragraph 3 notes that the IMO MSC-FAL.1/Circ.3 Guidelines on maritime cyber risk management (originally 5 July 2017; current revision Rev.2 of June 2022) provide high-level recommendations on maritime cyber risk management to safeguard shipping from current and emerging cyber threats. Implementation of these Guidelines is the primary evidence flag administrations and ROs review at DoC verification.

commonClauses.topic: Identify — threat environment, asset criticality, and cyber risk assessment
commonClauses.obligationText: Section 2.1 of MSC-FAL.1/Circ.3 Rev.2 (Identify) requires the Company to identify personnel and roles with cyber responsibilities, the threat environment relevant to the ship type and trade, the cyber-vulnerable shipboard systems (per §3.1 — bridge, propulsion, cargo, communications, passenger, administrative, access), and the risk that compromise of those systems poses to the safety of the ship, crew, passengers, cargo, environment, and the company. The output is a documented cyber risk register that drives the Protect / Detect / Respond / Recover functions.

commonClauses.topic: Protect — implementation of risk-control processes and measures
commonClauses.obligationText: Section 2.2 of MSC-FAL.1/Circ.3 Rev.2 (Protect) requires the Company to implement risk-control processes and measures, including but not limited to network segregation between IT and OT (operational technology — propulsion, navigation, cargo control, machinery management) systems, access control (least privilege, MFA for privileged remote access from shore), removable-media governance, malware protection, secure configuration baselines, and security patching. Controls must be commensurate to cyber risk register findings.

commonClauses.topic: Detect — cybersecurity event monitoring and incident-indicator activities
commonClauses.obligationText: Section 2.3 of MSC-FAL.1/Circ.3 Rev.2 (Detect) requires the Company to develop and implement activities necessary to identify the occurrence of a cyber event, including continuous monitoring of cyber-vulnerable shipboard systems, anomaly detection on OT control systems, integrity verification on safety-critical assets (ECDIS, GMDSS, AIS, propulsion control, dynamic positioning), and shore-side aggregation of vessel telemetry. Detection capability must be sufficient to support the 24-hour reporting clock for material cyber incidents to flag State, RO, and applicable port-State authority.

Section 2.4 of MSC-FAL.1/Circ.3 Rev.2 (Respond) requires the Company to develop and implement activities and plans to provide resilience and restore systems necessary for ship operation following a cyber-related event. Response plans must include shipboard and shore-side roles, an escalation matrix from the Master through the DPA (Designated Person Ashore) to the Cybersecurity Officer (CySO) and the Company Security Officer (CSO), incident classification (per BIMCO / company taxonomy), authority-notification timelines (flag administration, RO, USCG NRC for US waters, port-State authority, and class society for safety-significant events), and after-action documentation. Drills and tabletop exercises are required at least annually under ISM Code §8.

commonClauses.topic: Recover — recovery activities to restore safety-critical operations
commonClauses.obligationText: Section 2.5 of MSC-FAL.1/Circ.3 Rev.2 (Recover) requires the Company to identify activities to back up and restore cyber systems necessary for shipping operations impacted by a cyber event. Recovery plans must cover ECDIS / chart redundancy, GMDSS / AIS fallback (e.g. manual radio communications), propulsion-control fallback (manual engine room operation), navigation fallback (paper-chart-and-sextant if necessary), and shore-side cloud-restoration for office systems. Recovery activities must align with the Master's authority and the safety of life at sea (SOLAS) framework.

commonClauses.topic: Vulnerable shipboard systems — bridge, propulsion, cargo, communications, passenger, administrative, access
commonClauses.obligationText: Section 3.1 of MSC-FAL.1/Circ.3 Rev.2 lists seven categories of cyber-vulnerable shipboard systems that the Company must inventory and risk-assess: (a) bridge systems (ECDIS, IBS, INS, AIS, GMDSS, VDR, GPS/GNSS, dynamic-positioning HMI); (b) cargo-handling and management systems (TMS, cargo-control room, container bay plan, IG/HBV); (c) propulsion + machinery management + power-control systems (ECR, PMS, DPS, bow-thruster, rudder-steering control); (d) access-control systems (gangway, doors, CCTV, biometric); (e) passenger-servicing and management systems (HVAC, PA, billing/POS, IPTV); (f) passenger-facing public networks (passenger Wi-Fi); (g) administrative and crew-welfare systems (crew Wi-Fi, IPTV, BYOD).

Section 3.2 of MSC-FAL.1/Circ.3 Rev.2 requires the Company to consider cyber risks introduced by third parties, including OEMs of shipboard equipment, satellite-communications providers, chart-update services, IT-managed-service providers, classification societies (where they have remote-monitoring access), port-authority interfaces, and the broader maritime supply chain. Contractual cyber obligations must include vulnerability disclosure, SBOM / patch commitment, remote-access governance with session recording, and breach notification within company-defined timeframes (typically 24 hours).

commonClauses.topic: IACS UR E26 — cyber resilience of ships, design-level requirements
commonClauses.obligationText: IACS UR E26 (April 2022, applicable to ships contracted for newbuild construction on or after 1 July 2024) requires that ship newbuilds be designed with cyber resilience including documented inventory of CBS (cyber-resilient systems), network architecture with documented segregation between IT and OT zones, secure remote access (where present), security-event logging from CBS, malware protection on CBS, and a vulnerability- and patch-management process appropriate to the operational profile. Classification societies (DNV, ABS, Lloyd's, BV, KR, NK, RINA, CCS, IRS) verify E26 compliance at construction, with a class notation (e.g. DNV Cyber Secure, ABS CyberSafety, LR ShipRight Cyber).

commonClauses.topic: IACS UR E26 — access control, MFA for privileged remote access, removable-media governance
commonClauses.obligationText: Sub-clauses of IACS UR E26 require that access to CBS implement strong authentication (preferring MFA for privileged remote access from shore), least-privilege account management, audit logging of CBS access, and removable-media governance (USB sticks, optical media, and external drives must be controlled at engineering and bridge workstations through a documented process and may not be used to update ECDIS, GMDSS, or propulsion-control systems without an approved baseline check).

commonClauses.topic: IACS UR E26 — security-event monitoring on CBS
commonClauses.obligationText: IACS UR E26 requires that security-significant events on CBS be logged and made available for shipboard and shore-side review, with retention sufficient to support incident investigation (typically 90 days onboard, longer ashore). Monitoring covers authentication events, configuration changes, malware-detection events, network anomalies, and operator-action audit trails. Integration with shore-side SOC (Security Operations Centre) is encouraged.

commonClauses.topic: IACS UR E27 — cyber resilience of on-board systems and equipment (OEM type-approval)
commonClauses.obligationText: IACS UR E27 (April 2022, applicable to OT equipment integrated into ships contracted for newbuild on or after 1 July 2024) requires that on-board systems and equipment (engine OEM, navigation OEM, communications OEM, cargo-handling OEM, dynamic-positioning OEM, etc.) demonstrate cyber-resilience at product-type-approval level. Required security capabilities include identification + authentication, secure configuration, security event logging, malware protection (where applicable), backup + restore, and physical-tampering resistance. The classification society verifies E27 compliance during type-approval and integration into the ship.

commonClauses.topic: ISM Code — Safety Management System functional requirements (cyber integrated by MSC.428(98))
commonClauses.obligationText: ISM Code §1.4 (Functional requirements for an SMS) requires every company to establish, implement, and maintain an SMS that includes a safety and environmental-protection policy, instructions and procedures to ensure safe operation, defined levels of authority and lines of communication, procedures for reporting accidents, procedures to prepare for and respond to emergency situations, and procedures for internal audits and management reviews. MSC.428(98) extends each of these to include cyber risk; the SMS audit therefore includes cyber-incident-response procedures, cyber-event reporting lines, cyber-emergency drills, and cyber-related internal-audit findings.

commonClauses.topic: ISM Code — emergency-preparedness drills and exercises (including cyber)
commonClauses.obligationText: ISM Code §8.2 requires the Company to establish programmes for drills and exercises to prepare for emergency actions. Under MSC.428(98), cyber-incident drills (loss of ECDIS, GMDSS, AIS, propulsion control, or cargo-handling control through cyber compromise) must be part of the drill programme, at least annually fleet-wide, with documented after-action review and feedback into the SMS.

commonClauses.topic: BIMCO Guidelines — bridge-systems cyber considerations (ECDIS, GMDSS, AIS, VDR, GNSS)
commonClauses.obligationText: The BIMCO Guidelines on Cyber Security Onboard Ships (v4 June 2020; v5 forthcoming) provide implementation detail for cyber-vulnerable bridge systems. ECDIS and ENC chart updates must use authenticated update channels, GMDSS / AIS / VDES integrity must be monitored for jamming and spoofing, GNSS spoofing detection should be enabled, VDR (voyage data recorder) integrity must be preserved (the VDR is forensic evidence in cyber-incident investigations), and the Integrated Bridge System (IBS) configuration must be baselined with drift detection.

commonClauses.topic: BIMCO Guidelines — satcom and shore-to-ship remote-access governance
commonClauses.obligationText: BIMCO Guidelines require strict governance of shore-to-ship satcom links (VSAT, Iridium Certus, Inmarsat-FleetBroadband, Inmarsat-C). Remote-access sessions from shore-side IT / OEM support must use a PAM (Privileged Access Management) gateway with session recording, WYSIWYS (What-You-See-Is-What-You-Sign) confirmation for safety-significant changes, MFA on the shore side, and time-bounded access windows. Satcom traffic to OT systems (engine OEM remote monitoring, navigation OEM updates) must be segregated from administrative-LAN traffic and subject to deep-packet inspection where commercially feasible.

pendingChanges.subject: IMO Maritime Cyber Risk Management Review 2026 — possible MSC.428(98) successor with mandatory enforcement uplift
pendingChanges.status: The IMO Maritime Safety Committee is reviewing the operational experience with MSC.428(98) since 2021. Proposals under discussion (MSC.111+ session, expected 2026-2027) include making cyber-risk-managed SMS verification mandatory (rather than 'encouraged') for all flag administrations, harmonising reporting timelines across flag/port-State regimes, and aligning the IMO position with the IACS UR E26/E27 newbuild trajectory.

pendingChanges.subject: IACS UR E26 / E27 applicability extension — retrofit of existing fleet
pendingChanges.status: IACS UR E26 and E27 currently apply only to ships contracted for newbuild on or after 1 July 2024. Discussions are underway on extending equivalent expectations to existing fleet via class-society notation programmes (DNV Cyber Secure, ABS CyberSafety, LR ShipRight, BV Cyber Resilient) and via port-State control treating absence of CBS-inventory and CBS-monitoring as a major non-conformity.

pendingChanges.subject: BIMCO Guidelines v5 release expected 2024-2025
pendingChanges.status: BIMCO is updating its Guidelines on Cyber Security Onboard Ships from v4 (June 2020) to v5 (expected 2024-2025). Key updates include alignment with NIST CSF 2.0 (released February 2024), inclusion of IACS UR E26/E27 cross-references, expanded supply-chain guidance, and post-incident insurance-claim implications.

frameworks.name: RTCA DO-326A / EUROCAE ED-202A — Airworthiness Security Process Specification, plus DO-355A / ED-204A Information Security Guidance for Continuing Airworthiness, DO-356A / ED-203A Airworthiness Security Methods and Considerations, FAA AC 20-186, EASA AMC 20-42, and EASA Part-IS (Regulation (EU) 2022/1645 and 2023/203)
aliases: Information Security Guidance for Continuing Airworthiness

versions.grammarNotes: RTCA DO-326A 'Airworthiness Security Process Specification' (RTCA Inc., 6 August 2014) and the identical EUROCAE ED-202A (Edition 2, June 2014) are the joint US/EU industry standard that defines the airworthiness security certification process for civil aircraft. DO-326A is the primary basis on which the US Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) and the European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) issue type-certification cybersecurity findings for new aircraft (and for major modifications to existing types). DO-326A is supplemented by DO-356A 'Airworthiness Security Methods and Considerations' (June 2018) and DO-355A 'Information Security Guidance for Continuing Airworthiness' (June 2020 — the operator-side handoff). DO-391 'Aeronautical Information System Security Framework Guidance' (June 2019) and ED-205 extend the framework to aviation ground systems (airline ops centres, ATC, airport systems). FAA acceptance: AC 20-186 'Airworthiness and Operational Approval for Aircraft Network Security Program (ANSP)' (December 2020). EASA acceptance: AMC 20-42 'Airworthiness Information Security Risk Assessment' (CRD 2014-12, adopted 2020-2021) and now the binding EASA Part-IS regime — Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2022/1645 'Information Security Risks with a Potential Impact on Aviation Safety' and Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2023/203 (effective for all approved aviation organisations from 22 February 2026 — Part-21 design and production, Part-145 maintenance, Part-CAMO continuing airworthiness, Part-147 training, Part-ORA aerodromes, Part-OPS operators, etc.). The ARINC 811 aircraft information-security framework (defining the four aircraft information security domains — Aircraft Control Domain, Airline Information Services Domain, Passenger Information & Entertainment Services Domain, Passenger Owned Devices — referenced by DO-326A) is the architectural anchor. Catalogue clause IDs use 'DO-326A-sX-Y' for DO-326A sections, 'ED-202A-sX-Y' for the EUROCAE mirror, 'DO-355A-sX-Y' for the continuing-airworthiness companion, 'DO-356A-sX-Y' for the methods companion, 'FAA-AC-20-186-sN' for FAA Advisory Circular sections, 'EASA-AMC-20-42-sN' for EASA AMC sections, 'EASA-Part-IS-IS-OR-NNN' for the Part-IS Implementing Rules (mirroring the regulation paragraph numbering), and 'ARINC-811-sX-Y' for ARINC 811 security-domain references.

commonClauses.topic: Airworthiness security objectives — preventing intentional unauthorized electronic interaction with aircraft safety
commonClauses.obligationText: Section 2.1 of DO-326A defines the objective of airworthiness security: to protect the aircraft from intentional unauthorized electronic interaction (IUEI) that could cause a failure condition with safety effect equal to or greater than 'minor' (CS 25.1309 / Part 25.1309). The applicant for a type certificate (or major modification) must demonstrate that the aircraft's airborne systems, when integrated with their data interfaces, cannot be compromised by IUEI in a manner that affects continued safe flight and landing. This is the umbrella objective that drives every subsequent process step.

commonClauses.topic: Plan for Security Aspects of Certification (PSecAA) — project-level security plan
commonClauses.obligationText: Section 2.2 of DO-326A requires the applicant to develop and maintain a Plan for Security Aspects of Certification (PSecAA). The PSecAA documents how the airworthiness-security process will be conducted for the type certification (or major modification) project: scope, applicable Cyber Security Items, security-process activities, organisation and responsibilities, applicable standards (DO-326A, DO-356A, plus AC 20-186 / AMC 20-42), security verification and validation activities, configuration management and quality assurance for security artefacts, and the security plan for in-service support (linkage to DO-355A). The certification authority (FAA / EASA / TCCA / CAA / ANAC / JCAB) reviews and accepts the PSecAA early in the project; non-conformance with the PSecAA can result in a finding of non-compliance with the certification basis.

commonClauses.topic: Aircraft Security Scope — Cyber Security Items (CSIs) identification
commonClauses.obligationText: Section 3.1 of DO-326A requires the applicant to identify all Cyber Security Items (CSIs) within the aircraft type design. CSIs include airborne electronic hardware, airborne software, network components, data buses, interfaces with off-aircraft systems (ACARS, CPDLC, ADS-B, GNSS, ATC, EFB, IFE, satcom), loadable software aircraft parts (LSAPs), and the related ground-based tools used for design, production, and continuing airworthiness. The applicant must maintain an authoritative CSI register, updated through configuration management, and trace each CSI to its applicable threat conditions, security controls, and verification evidence. The CSI register is part of the type-certificate data package.

commonClauses.topic: Threat Conditions Identification (TCI) — threat scenarios with safety effect
commonClauses.obligationText: Section 3.2 of DO-326A requires the applicant to identify Threat Conditions (TCs) — credible scenarios in which an attacker could exploit a CSI to cause a failure condition with safety effect. Threat conditions include malware introduction via maintenance laptop, ACARS / CPDLC message injection, GNSS spoofing, ADS-B injection, EFB performance-data manipulation, IFE-to-avionics traversal (the 'crossover' threat documented in the FAA's 2015 Special Conditions for the Boeing 787), engine-OEM remote-monitoring channel abuse, and supply-chain compromise of LSAPs. Each TC is classified by safety effect (No Safety Effect / Minor / Major / Hazardous / Catastrophic) per the CS 25.1309 / Part 25.1309 framework, and is the entry point to the security risk assessment.

commonClauses.topic: Security Risk Assessment (SRA) — likelihood × consequence per threat condition
commonClauses.obligationText: Section 3.3 of DO-326A requires the applicant to perform a Security Risk Assessment (SRA) for each identified Threat Condition. The SRA combines the likelihood of successful exploitation (considering attacker capability, attack-path complexity, and CSI exposure) with the consequence (safety effect classification) to produce a residual security risk per TC. Risks that exceed the acceptable threshold (typically anything Major / Hazardous / Catastrophic with non-Minimal likelihood) must be mitigated by security controls and re-assessed until residual risk is acceptable. The SRA is documented and traceable from the TC list through controls to verification evidence. The methods for performing the SRA are specified in DO-356A / ED-203A.

commonClauses.topic: Security Architecture and Design — countermeasures, network segregation, ARINC 811 domains
commonClauses.obligationText: Section 4.1 of DO-326A requires the applicant to define and document the aircraft security architecture, including the segregation of the four ARINC 811 aircraft information security domains: (a) Aircraft Control Domain (ACD) — flight controls, FMS, avionics, engine-control, hydraulics; highest assurance; (b) Airline Information Services Domain (AISD) — EFB host, ACMS, CMU, AID; (c) Passenger Information & Entertainment Services Domain (PIESD) — IFE, passenger Wi-Fi, seat-back screens; (d) Passenger Owned Devices (POD) — passenger BYOD; lowest trust. Information may flow from higher to lower trust domains; flow in the other direction must be either prohibited or mediated by an evaluated security boundary device (network guard, data diode, controlled-interface device). The design is verified at integration test.

commonClauses.topic: Security Effectiveness Demonstration — verification of countermeasures
commonClauses.obligationText: Section 4.2 of DO-326A requires the applicant to verify the effectiveness of each security control against the threat conditions it mitigates. Verification methods include security testing (functional, vulnerability, penetration), security analysis (code review, design review), and equivalent processes traceable through the security plan. Verification evidence is documented in the Security Assurance Plan and submitted as part of the type-certificate data package. The certification authority reviews this evidence as part of the airworthiness security finding under CS 25.1309 / Part 25.1309 (or equivalent for rotorcraft, engines, propellers).

commonClauses.topic: Continued Airworthiness Security Information (CASI) — handoff to operator under DO-355A
commonClauses.obligationText: Section 5.1 of DO-326A requires the type-certificate holder (TC-holder — Boeing, Airbus, Embraer, ATR, Bombardier, etc.) to develop Continued Airworthiness Security Information (CASI) that is delivered to operators along with the aircraft. CASI is the security analogue of the Instructions for Continued Airworthiness (ICA) under Part 25.1529 / CS 25.1529 and includes the security configuration baselines, the list of LSAPs and their integrity-verification process, the maintenance-laptop / PMAT security requirements, the security-event monitoring requirements, the in-service vulnerability handling process, and the cyber-incident reporting interface. Operators implement CASI under DO-355A.

commonClauses.topic: DO-355A — operator information security activities for continuing airworthiness
commonClauses.obligationText: Section 2.1 of DO-355A (June 2020) defines the operator-side activities for continued airworthiness security: monitor cyber threats relevant to the airframe type, maintain the CSI inventory in-service, verify LSAP integrity at load time, audit maintenance-laptop / PMAT security posture, track Service Bulletins and Airworthiness Directives for security relevance, perform operator security risk reviews on change, train flight and maintenance crew on cyber events, respond to cyber incidents per the response plan, and report cyber incidents to the TC-holder and the competent authority (FAA / EASA NCA). DO-355A is the regulatory hook through which operators inherit the type-certificate cybersecurity obligations.

commonClauses.topic: DO-355A — Loadable Software Aircraft Part (LSAP) integrity verification at load time
commonClauses.obligationText: Section 2.3 of DO-355A requires the operator to verify the integrity (digital signature or hash) of every Loadable Software Aircraft Part (LSAP) at the time of load into the aircraft. LSAPs include navigation databases, FMS databases, terrain databases, performance databases, applicable user-modifiable software, and field-loadable airborne software. The verification process must use the TC-holder's authoritative signing keys, must be performed by a controlled tool chain (the PMAT / data-loader), and must record an audit event that links the LSAP version, the digital-signature verification result, the loading technician, the date/time, and the aircraft tail number. Failed verifications must block the load and trigger an incident.

commonClauses.topic: DO-355A — cyber-incident detection, response, and reporting (operator obligations)
commonClauses.obligationText: Section 3.1 of DO-355A requires the operator to detect, respond to, and report cyber-incidents that affect (or could affect) airworthiness. Detection includes monitoring the security-relevant events emitted by the airborne CSIs (engine OEM data, ACARS / CPDLC integrity, EFB host events, maintenance-laptop events), and shore-side monitoring of operator IT systems with safety relevance (flight-planning, performance-calculation, dispatch). Response includes isolating the affected aircraft from the operational fleet pending investigation, notifying the TC-holder, coordinating with the maintenance organisation, and (where required) escalating to the competent authority. Reporting timelines under EASA Part-IS are 24 hours initial / 72 hours preliminary / 1 month final to the National Competent Authority (NCA) and EASA.

commonClauses.topic: DO-356A — methods for security risk assessment (qualitative + quantitative)
commonClauses.obligationText: Section 3.1 of DO-356A (June 2018) provides the methods by which the security risk assessment from DO-326A §3.3 is performed. The standard recognises attack-tree analysis, threat / risk / vulnerability analysis, qualitative scoring matrices, and quantitative likelihood/consequence calculation as acceptable methods. The method chosen must be documented in the PSecAA, must be repeatable, and must produce evidence sufficient for the certification authority to make an independent finding. DO-356A also specifies the documentation conventions (Security Operating Concept, Threat Conditions list, Security Risk Register, Security Architecture Description) that form the type-certificate data package.

commonClauses.topic: FAA AC 20-186 — Acceptance of DO-326A / DO-355A / DO-356A as a means of compliance
commonClauses.obligationText: Section 5 of FAA Advisory Circular AC 20-186 'Airworthiness and Operational Approval for Aircraft Network Security Program (ANSP)' (December 2020) accepts DO-326A, DO-355A, and DO-356A as acceptable means of compliance for the Aircraft Network Security Program (ANSP) demonstration required by an applicant for FAA type certification (or supplemental type certificate, STC) of an aircraft with networked airborne systems. AC 20-186 also instructs FAA Aircraft Certification Service (AIR) engineers to use DO-326A as the framework for cybersecurity findings in lieu of project-specific Special Conditions (which had historically been used on Boeing 787, Airbus A350, Bombardier Global 7500, Embraer E-Jet E2, and others). Operators inherit FAA acceptance via the operator's Continued Airworthiness Maintenance Program.

commonClauses.topic: EASA AMC 20-42 — Airworthiness Information Security Risk Assessment (acceptance of ED-202A)
commonClauses.obligationText: Section 4 of EASA Acceptable Means of Compliance AMC 20-42 'Airworthiness Information Security Risk Assessment' (CRD 2014-12, adopted 2020-2021) accepts EUROCAE ED-202A as an acceptable means of compliance for the airworthiness security objectives of CS 25.1309 (large aeroplanes), CS 27.1309 (small rotorcraft), CS 29.1309 (large rotorcraft), and CS-E (engines). EASA Aircraft Certification Department uses AMC 20-42 in concert with ED-203A (methods) and ED-204A (continuing airworthiness) to make cybersecurity findings on new type certificates and major modifications. AMC 20-42 also references the ARINC 811 aircraft information security domains as the architectural baseline.

commonClauses.topic: EASA Part-IS IS.OR.200 — Information Security Management System (ISMS) scope
commonClauses.obligationText: IS.OR.200 of Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2022/1645 + Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2023/203 'Part-IS' (effective for all approved aviation organisations from 22 February 2026) requires every approved organisation under Part-21 (design and production), Part-145 (maintenance), Part-CAMO (continuing airworthiness management), Part-147 (training), Part-ORA (aerodromes), Part-OPS (operators), Part-ATM/ANS, and Part-ATCO to establish, implement, maintain, and continuously improve an Information Security Management System (ISMS). The ISMS scope must cover information and systems that, if compromised, could have an impact on aviation safety. The ISMS must be aligned with the organisation's management system and integrated into safety management. This is the binding regulatory hook through which DO-326A / DO-355A processes become legal obligations in EU/EEA.

commonClauses.topic: EASA Part-IS IS.OR.205 — Information security risk assessment
commonClauses.obligationText: IS.OR.205 of Part-IS requires every approved organisation to perform an information-security risk assessment that identifies the information security risks affecting aviation safety, assesses likelihood and consequence, and documents the risk-treatment decisions. The risk assessment must be reviewed at least annually and after any significant change. For TC-holders and DOA (Design Organisation Approval) holders, this aligns with DO-326A §3.3; for operators and CAMO organisations, this aligns with DO-355A operator-side risk review.

commonClauses.topic: EASA Part-IS IS.OR.220 — Information security incidents (detection, response, recovery)
commonClauses.obligationText: IS.OR.220 of Part-IS requires every approved organisation to detect, respond to, and recover from information security incidents that could affect aviation safety. The organisation must have a documented incident-response process, must train personnel on its application, and must maintain records of incidents and lessons-learned. Detection capability must be commensurate with the organisation's risk profile (a large operator with a global fleet and AOC will require shore-side SOC capability; a small DOA may rely on cloud services with documented monitoring).

commonClauses.topic: EASA Part-IS IS.OR.230 — External reporting scheme (24h / 72h / 1 month)
commonClauses.obligationText: IS.OR.230 of Part-IS requires every approved organisation to report information security incidents that could affect aviation safety to its National Competent Authority (NCA) and to EASA. Reporting timelines mirror the EU NIS2 cadence: an initial notification within 24 hours of becoming aware (with the indication of whether the incident is suspected to be of malicious origin and whether it could have cross-border effect), a preliminary report within 72 hours, and a final report within 1 month. NCA and EASA may share information with the EU CSIRTs network and with the EU Aviation Safety reporting scheme. Reporting is in addition to (not in place of) Air Operator Certificate occurrence reporting under Regulation (EU) 376/2014.

commonClauses.topic: EASA Part-IS IS.OR.235 — Contracting of activities (supply-chain ISMS flow-down)
commonClauses.obligationText: IS.OR.235 of Part-IS requires every approved organisation that contracts activities relevant to information security to ensure that the contractor meets information-security requirements equivalent to those that apply to the contracting organisation. This includes IT-managed-service providers, cloud-service providers (especially where flight-planning, performance-calculation, or dispatch software is operated), engine and avionics OEM remote-monitoring providers, ground-handling system providers, and EFB content providers. The contracting organisation remains responsible for the safety effect of any compromise originating with a contractor.

IS.OR.245 of Part-IS requires every approved organisation to keep records of the ISMS — risk assessment, risk-treatment plan, security controls, incident records, audit findings, training records, contractor flow-down evidence — for a minimum of 5 years after the activity to which the record relates. Records must be available to the NCA and to EASA on request. For TC-holders, the retention period typically aligns with the type-certificate lifecycle (i.e. decades).

commonClauses.topic: ARINC 811 — aircraft information security domains (ACD / AISD / PIESD / POD)
commonClauses.obligationText: Section 4.2 of ARINC 811 'Commercial Aircraft Information Security Concepts of Operation and Process Framework' (December 2005, current revision 2017) defines the four aircraft information security domains that are the architectural anchor of DO-326A §4.1: (a) Aircraft Control Domain (ACD) — the avionics core (flight controls, FMS, autopilot, autothrottle, navigation, engine-control, hydraulics, fuel, electrical, environmental); requires the highest level of integrity and availability; only crew-cockpit interaction. (b) Airline Information Services Domain (AISD) — operator-installed equipment that supports flight operations (EFB host, ACMS, central maintenance computer, AID — Aircraft Interface Device, Wi-Fi access points for crew use). (c) Passenger Information and Entertainment Services Domain (PIESD) — in-flight entertainment, passenger Wi-Fi access points, seatback screens, food / merchandise POS, cellular pico-cells. (d) Passenger Owned Devices (POD) — passenger BYOD devices on the cabin Wi-Fi. Inter-domain information flow is asymmetric: read-only from higher to lower trust is generally permitted, but write or any flow from lower to higher trust must be either prohibited or mediated by an evaluated security boundary device.

pendingChanges.subject: EASA Part-IS effective date — 22 February 2026 (binding for all approved aviation organisations)
pendingChanges.status: Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2022/1645 and Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2023/203 become binding for all approved EU/EEA aviation organisations on 22 February 2026. National Competent Authorities (BAZL Switzerland, DGCA France, LBA Germany, ENAC Italy, AESA Spain, CAA UK in alignment, etc.) are issuing transposition guidance through 2025-2026. Organisations that have not transitioned by the effective date face suspension of approvals. ISMS audits become part of the standard organisation audit cycle.

pendingChanges.subject: ICAO Annex 17 cybersecurity amendments — 2026-2027 expected
pendingChanges.status: ICAO is updating Annex 17 (Security) to include cybersecurity provisions that align with DO-326A / Part-IS / NIS2 in a globally-harmonised manner. The amendments are expected to be adopted by the ICAO Council in 2026 and to come into effect in 2027. Once in force, ICAO Contracting States (193 nations) must include cybersecurity in their National Civil Aviation Security Programme (NCASP).

pendingChanges.subject: FAA Aviation Cybersecurity NPRM (Notice of Proposed Rulemaking) — 2026 expected
pendingChanges.status: The FAA is preparing a Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM) that would codify aircraft network security program requirements (currently in AC 20-186) as binding regulation under 14 CFR Part 25, Part 121, Part 135. The NPRM is expected in 2026 and would align FAA practice with EASA Part-IS where commercially reasonable.

derivesFrom.$comment: Derivative-regulation graph: each key is a framework id, value is the parent framework it inherits mappings from plus per-clause divergences and an explicit clauseMapping when the derivative renumbers clauses. inheritanceMode controls propagation: 'identity' (derivative preserves parent clause numbers — any parent clause propagates 1:1 unless listed in divergences) or 'mapped' (derivative renumbers — only clauses listed in clauseMapping propagate). scripts/generate_phase3_3_derivatives.py reads this graph to materialise inherited compliance[] entries on UC sidecars, with assurance degraded one step (full → partial → contributing; contributing does not propagate further). Divergences carry an informational note that is attached to the inherited entry via derivationSource.divergenceNote for auditor review.

uk-gdpr.$identityComment: UK GDPR was formed by onshoring Regulation (EU) 2016/679 into UK law under the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018. Clause numbering is preserved 1:1 with EU GDPR; any parent Art.N propagates to UK-GDPR@Art.N unless listed in divergences.

divergences.note: UK adequacy decisions managed by ICO/UK government, not EU Commission.
UK's own international cooperation mechanisms apply.

clauseMapping.$comment: parent-clause → derivative-clause. Swiss nFADP uses its own article numbering but covers substantially the same topics as GDPR. Mapping is hand-curated against the FDPIC's 2023 nFADP commentary.

divergences.note: nFADP applies 'good faith' principle; GDPR uses 'lawfulness, fairness, transparency'.
Swiss FDPIC replaces EU supervisory authority for breach notification.

clauseMapping.$comment: parent-clause → derivative-clause. LGPD (Lei nº 13.709/2018) closely mirrors GDPR but renumbers security and breach articles. Mapping verified against ANPD's 2022 guidance.

divergences.note: LGPD Art.7 enumerates 10 legal bases; slightly different from GDPR's 6.
ANPD is the competent authority; notification timelines differ.

clauseMapping.$comment: parent-clause → derivative-clause. APPI (2022 amendments) uses its own article numbering. Mapping verified against PPC's APPI implementation guidelines.

divergences.note: APPI uses 'purpose of utilisation' rather than GDPR's lawful bases.
APPI data-subject rights are narrower than GDPR Art.15-22.

clauseMapping.$comment: parent-clause → derivative-clause. CCPA/CPRA uses §-based Civil Code numbering and is a consumer-privacy statute rather than a comprehensive data-protection regime; only the three commonClauses are mapped. Divergences list still applies: inherited entries gain a divergenceNote so SMEs can flag scope mismatches (e.g. employee data, sale/share definitions).

divergences.note: Right to know is scoped to California residents and consumer context, not employees in most cases.
Right to delete includes service-provider obligations explicit in CCPA §1798.105.
CCPA is opt-out, not opt-in; consent not required to collect in most cases.

aliasIndex.$comment: Flat alias → framework-id map used by scripts/gap-analysis and scripts/audit_compliance_mappings to normalise free-text 'Regulations:' strings in legacy markdown. Unknown aliases appear in docs/content-gap-analysis.md.
